diagram_description: Graphical representation of sound wave showing wavelength, amplitude, crests, and troughs on density vs. distance plot
subject: physics

summary: Wavelength (λ) is the distance between two consecutive crests or two consecutive troughs of a wave, measured in metres. Short wavelengths correspond to high-frequency sounds, while long wavelengths correspond to low-frequency sounds.
diagram_description: Wave graph showing distance between consecutive crests labeled as wavelength λ
subject: physics

summary: Frequency (ν) is the number of density oscillations per unit time at a fixed point, measured in hertz (Hz). Time period (T) is the time for one complete oscillation. Frequency and time period are inversely related: ν = 1/T. Higher frequency corresponds to shorter time period.
diagram_description: Graph showing density oscillations vs. time at a fixed point with one complete cycle marked as time period T
subject: physics

summary: Amplitude is the maximum change in air density in a compression or rarefaction compared to average density. Intensity is the amount of sound energy passing through a unit area perpendicular to wave propagation per unit time. Larger amplitude corresponds to higher intensity and greater energy transfer.
diagram_description: Two wave graphs showing low amplitude vs. high amplitude sound waves with amplitude marked vertically
subject: physics

summary: Speed of sound (v) is the distance a wave point (such as a crest) travels per unit time. It depends on the medium: approximately 340 m/s in air at 22 °C, 1500 m/s in water, and 5000 m/s in steel. Speed is related to wavelength and frequency by v = λν.
diagram_description: Table comparing speeds of sound in different media (solids, liquids, gases) with numerical values
subject: physics

summary: Human perception of sound is subjective and described by pitch (how shrill or deep a sound is) and loudness (how intense a sound is). Pitch correlates with frequency, while loudness correlates with amplitude. Humans can hear sounds in the range 20 Hz to 20 kHz, but this range varies with age and individual.
diagram_description: Illustration of the human ear showing eardrum and cochlea converting sound vibrations to neural signals
subject: physics

summary: Pitch is how frequency is perceived by humans. High-pitch sounds (shrill, like a whistle or siren) have higher frequency; low-pitch sounds (deep, like thunder) have lower frequency. The exact relationship between frequency and perceived pitch is complex but generally follows the pattern that higher frequency yields higher pitch.
diagram_description: Frequency spectrum showing low-pitch (low frequency) to high-pitch (high frequency) sound waves
subject: physics

summary: Loudness is the human perception of sound amplitude. Sounds with larger amplitude are perceived as louder; those with smaller amplitude sound softer. Loudness is measured in decibels (dB): normal conversation is about 60 dB, while loud sounds like firecrackers exceed 100 dB.
diagram_description: Scale showing decibel levels from very soft sounds (rustling leaves) to very loud sounds (firecrackers and jets)
subject: physics

summary: Humans can hear sound frequencies from 20 Hz to 20 kHz (20,000 Hz), though this range decreases with age. Frequencies below 20 Hz are called infrasonic; frequencies above 20 kHz are called ultrasonic. Dogs, bats, and dolphins can detect ultrasound; elephants can detect infrasound.
diagram_description: Frequency spectrum showing human audible range (20 Hz–20 kHz) with infrasonic and ultrasonic regions marked
subject: physics

summary: Sound waves reflect off obstacles following the same laws of reflection as light: the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection. Common manifestations include echoes (single reflection from distant surfaces) and reverberations (multiple reflections arriving within 0.05 s). Echo requires minimum distance of 17 m for human perception.
diagram_description: Ray diagram showing sound reflecting off a hard surface with incident and reflected rays at equal angles to the normal
subject: physics

summary: Sound waves bounce off hard and smooth surfaces. The laws of reflection state that the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection, both measured from the normal to the surface. Soft surfaces absorb sound; rough surfaces scatter it. Echoes are strongest from hard, smooth, distant surfaces.
diagram_description: Diagram showing incident sound ray and reflected sound ray with equal angles to the normal surface
subject: physics

summary: An echo is a sound reflection heard as a separate sound from the original. For echoes to be heard distinctly, the time gap between original and reflected sound must be at least 0.1 s, corresponding to a minimum distance of approximately 17 m for the reflecting surface. Hard surfaces like mountains and cliffs produce clear echoes.
diagram_description: Diagram showing sound traveling to a distant wall and reflecting back, with time delay marked between source and echo
subject: physics

summary: Reverberation is the persistence of sound after the source stops emitting, caused by multiple reflections arriving with time differences less than 0.05 s. In auditoriums, sound-absorbing materials (curtains, upholstered seats, porous panels) reduce unwanted reverberations while maintaining desirable acoustic qualities.
diagram_description: Cross-section of auditorium showing sound waves reflecting multiple times from walls, ceiling, and floor
subject: physics

summary: Infrasonic waves (below 20 Hz) and ultrasonic waves (above 20 kHz) are outside human hearing range but have important applications. Infrasonic waves detect earthquakes and storms; ultrasonic waves are used in medical imaging, industrial cleaning, and echolocation. These frequencies extend the utility of sound beyond human perception.
diagram_description: Frequency spectrum showing infrasonic, audible, and ultrasonic regions with applications labeled
subject: physics

summary: Infrasonic waves are sound waves with frequency below 20 Hz, inaudible to humans. They are used to detect natural events such as earthquakes and volcanic eruptions, and to detect severe storms. Elephants and some other animals can detect infrasound for communication over long distances.
diagram_description: Application diagram showing infrasonic waves from earthquakes and storms being detected by monitoring equipment
subject: physics

summary: Ultrasonic waves are sound waves with frequency above 20 kHz, inaudible to humans. Applications include medical ultrasonography (imaging internal organs), breaking kidney stones (lithotripsy), ultrasonic welding, cleaning delicate parts, detecting defects in metals, and echolocation. Dogs, bats, and dolphins naturally produce and detect ultrasound.
diagram_description: Diagram showing ultrasound transducer producing ultrasonic waves used in medical ultrasound imaging of organs
subject: physics

summary: Echolocation is the ability to locate objects using reflected sound waves. Bats emit short bursts of ultrasonic waves and sense the echoes to determine position and distance of obstacles and prey. Dolphins, whales, and some birds also use echolocation for navigation and hunting. Humans have adapted this principle in sonar technology.
diagram_description: Diagram of a bat emitting ultrasonic waves and detecting reflected echoes from a flying insect
subject: physics

summary: Sound is a form of energy. When a sound source vibrates, it transfers energy to the surrounding medium. As sound waves propagate, the vibration of particles and their collisions result in energy transfer. Larger amplitude waves carry more energy and can travel farther before intensity reduces to zero.
diagram_description: Diagram showing grains jumping on a membrane due to sound vibrations from a vibrating plate, demonstrating energy transfer
subject: physics

summary: A transverse wave is a wave in which particles vibrate in a direction perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation. Light is an example of a transverse wave and can travel through vacuum. This contrasts with sound, which is a longitudinal wave.
diagram_description: Wave diagram showing particle displacement perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation (left to right)
subject: physics

summary: A sonic boom is a loud sound produced when a supersonic aircraft (traveling faster than the speed of sound) creates a sudden disturbance in air density. Similar loud sound pulses occur when air is heated rapidly (e.g., thunder from lightning). The disturbance propagates outward as a shock wave.
diagram_description: Diagram showing a supersonic aircraft with shock wave cone surrounding it representing sonic boom generation
subject: physics

summary: Timbre is the quality that distinguishes sounds of different instruments even when played at the same loudness and pitch. It arises from the pattern and intensity of overtones (higher frequencies) that accompany the fundamental frequency. Shape, material, and construction of an instrument determine its timbre.
diagram_description: Frequency spectrum graphs comparing a pure tone (tuning fork) with a musical note (musical instrument) showing overtones
subject: physics

summary: Sonar (Sound Navigation and Ranging) is an underwater exploration technique using ultrasonic waves. Ultrasonic waves are sent into water and reflected waves are analyzed to determine distance, direction, and speed of underwater objects. Applications include locating submarines, shipwrecks, and measuring ocean depth.
diagram_description: Diagram showing a ship sending ultrasonic waves downward to detect a submarine, with reflected waves returning
subject: physics

summary: Noise is unwanted or harmful sound. Noise pollution occurs when exposure to sound levels above recommended limits affects health, sleep, and hearing. Prolonged exposure to loud sound (above 80 dB) can lead to hearing loss. Hearing aids consisting of microphone, amplifier, and speaker can assist people with hearing impairment.
diagram_description: Chart showing typical sound levels from various sources and their decibel ratings, with safe vs. harmful levels marked
subject: physics

concept_name: Speed of Sound in Different Conditions
summary: The speed of sound in air depends on temperature and humidity. Speed increases with increasing temperature or humidity. In dry air, speed is approximately 331 m/s at 0 °C and 344 m/s at 22 °C. In most media, speed depends only on the medium, not on the frequency of the source.
diagram_description: Graph showing speed of sound versus temperature in air, with a linear positive relationship
subject: physics

summary: Reflection is the bouncing back of light from a surface. It follows two laws: the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection, and the incident ray, normal, and reflected ray lie in the same plane. These laws apply to all reflecting surfaces including mirrors.
diagram_description: Diagram showing incident ray, normal, and reflected ray at a reflecting surface with labeled angles
subject: physics

summary: The laws of reflection state: (1) angle of incidence equals angle of reflection, and (2) the incident ray, normal, and reflected ray all lie in the same plane. These laws hold for all types of reflecting surfaces.
diagram_description: Ray diagram at a reflecting surface showing incident ray, normal, and reflected ray with equal angles marked
subject: physics

summary: A plane mirror always forms a virtual, erect image equal in size to the object. The image is as far behind the mirror as the object is in front and is laterally inverted. No real image is formed by a plane mirror.
diagram_description: Diagram of plane mirror with object and virtual image on opposite sides equidistant from mirror surface
subject: physics

summary: Spherical mirrors are curved mirrors with reflecting surfaces forming part of a sphere. A concave mirror (curves inward) converges light rays to a focus and can form real or virtual images. A convex mirror (curves outward) diverges light rays and always forms virtual, diminished images.
diagram_description: Side-by-side ray diagrams showing concave mirror converging rays and convex mirror diverging rays to/from focal points
subject: physics

summary: The mirror formula relates object distance (u), image distance (v), and focal length (f) by: 1/v + 1/u = 1/f. Magnification m = -v/u indicates the ratio of image to object height, where negative m indicates a real inverted image and positive m indicates a virtual erect image.
diagram_description: Labeled diagram of spherical mirror with object, image, focal point, and center of curvature marked with distances
subject: physics

summary: The New Cartesian Sign Convention takes the mirror pole as origin and principal axis as x-axis. Distances measured to the right (away from mirror) are positive, to the left (toward mirror) are negative. Distances above the axis are positive, below are negative.
diagram_description: Coordinate system diagram showing spherical mirror with pole at origin, principal axis, and sign convention for all four quadrants
subject: physics

summary: Refraction is the bending of light when it travels obliquely from one transparent medium to another. Light bends toward the normal when entering a denser medium and away from the normal when entering a rarer medium. The extent of bending depends on the refractive indices of the media.
diagram_description: Ray diagram showing light ray bending at the interface between two transparent media with incident angle and refraction angle marked
subject: physics

summary: The laws of refraction state: (1) the incident ray, refracted ray, and normal lie in the same plane, and (2) the ratio sin(i)/sin(r) is constant for a given pair of media (Snell's Law). This constant is the refractive index of the second medium with respect to the first.
diagram_description: Ray diagram at a refracting surface showing incident ray, refracted ray, normal, and labeled angles of incidence and refraction

builds_upon.concept_name: Speed of Light in Different Media

subject: physics

summary: The refractive index of a medium is the ratio of the speed of light in vacuum to its speed in that medium: n = c/v. A medium with higher refractive index is optically denser and slows light more. Refractive index determines how much light bends at an interface.
diagram_description: Table or graph showing refractive indices of various materials arranged from lowest to highest

builds_upon.concept_name: Speed of Light in Different Media

subject: physics

summary: When light passes through a rectangular glass slab, refraction occurs at both the entry (air-glass) and exit (glass-air) surfaces. The emergent ray is parallel to the incident ray but laterally displaced. The extent of bending at opposite parallel surfaces is equal and opposite.
diagram_description: Ray diagram showing light entering a rectangular glass slab at an angle, bending at the first surface, traveling through the slab, and emerging parallel to the incident ray with lateral displacement shown
subject: physics

summary: A lens is a transparent optical element with one or two spherical surfaces. A convex (converging) lens is thicker at the center and focuses parallel light rays to a point. A concave (diverging) lens is thicker at the edges and diverges parallel rays as if from a point.
diagram_description: Side-by-side diagrams of convex and concave lenses showing their shape, principal axis, optical center, focal points, and how parallel rays behave
subject: physics

summary: The lens formula relates object distance (u), image distance (v), and focal length (f) by: 1/v - 1/u = 1/f. Magnification m = v/u indicates the ratio of image to object height, where negative m indicates a real inverted image and positive m indicates a virtual erect image.
diagram_description: Labeled diagram of a spherical lens with object on one side, image on the other, focal points, and distances marked
subject: physics

summary: The power of a lens is the reciprocal of its focal length: P = 1/f, measured in dioptres (D). Convex lenses have positive power, concave lenses have negative power. Higher power indicates greater ability to converge or diverge light.
diagram_description: Diagram showing lenses of different focal lengths with corresponding powers, indicating the relationship between focal length and power
subject: physics

summary: A concave mirror forms different types of images depending on object position. Beyond 2F: diminished real inverted image. At 2F: same-sized real inverted image. Between F and 2F: magnified real inverted image. Between P and F: magnified virtual erect image. At F: no image.
diagram_description: Comprehensive diagram showing concave mirror with marked positions P, F, C (2F) and ray diagrams for each object position
subject: physics

summary: Image position, size, and nature vary with object position in concave mirrors. Objects at infinity form point-sized real images at focus; beyond C form diminished images between F and C; at C form same-sized images at C; between C and F form enlarged images beyond C.
diagram_description: Table or diagram showing all six object positions for a concave mirror with corresponding image positions and properties
subject: physics

summary: Ray diagrams for concave mirrors use two convenient rays: one parallel to the principal axis reflects through focus, and one through focus reflects parallel to axis. The intersection of two reflected rays locates the image. A ray through the center of curvature reflects back along itself.
diagram_description: Ray diagram showing concave mirror with multiple incident rays, their reflections, and image formation by ray intersection
subject: physics

summary: Concave mirrors are used in torches, search-lights, and vehicle headlights to produce parallel beams. Shaving and dental mirrors use the magnifying property. Large concave mirrors in solar furnaces concentrate sunlight to generate heat.
diagram_description: Practical applications showing concave mirrors in headlights, shaving mirrors, dental equipment, and solar furnaces
subject: physics

summary: A convex mirror always forms a virtual, erect, and diminished image regardless of object position. The image is formed behind the mirror between the pole and focus. Convex mirrors provide a wider field of view than plane mirrors.
diagram_description: Diagram showing convex mirror with object on one side and virtual image appearing behind the mirror with rays illustrated
subject: physics

summary: For a convex mirror, objects at infinity form point-sized virtual images at focus. Objects at finite distances form virtual images between pole and focus, erect and diminished. Image position moves closer to pole as object moves away from mirror.
diagram_description: Table or diagram showing convex mirror object positions and corresponding virtual image positions behind the mirror
subject: physics

summary: For convex mirrors, a ray parallel to the principal axis appears to diverge from the focus after reflection. A ray directed toward the focus diverges parallel to the axis. A ray aimed at the center of curvature reflects back along itself. Virtual image is located where reflected rays appear to originate.
diagram_description: Ray diagram for convex mirror showing parallel ray diverging from focus and virtual image formation behind mirror
subject: physics

summary: Convex mirrors are used as rear-view mirrors in vehicles because they always produce an erect, diminished image and provide a wider field of view. This allows drivers to see more of the traffic behind them, enhancing safety.
diagram_description: Diagram showing a convex mirror on a vehicle with the wider field of view compared to a plane mirror illustrated
subject: physics

summary: A convex lens forms different image types depending on object position. At infinity: point-sized real image at focus. Beyond 2F: diminished real inverted image. At 2F: same-sized real inverted image. Between F and 2F: magnified real inverted image. Between F and O: magnified virtual erect image.
diagram_description: Comprehensive diagram showing convex lens with marked positions 2F1, F1, O, F2, 2F2 and ray diagrams for multiple object positions
subject: physics

summary: For convex lenses, image characteristics change with object position. Objects at infinity form point-sized images at focus; beyond 2F form diminished images between F and 2F; at 2F form same-sized images at 2F; between F and 2F form enlarged images beyond 2F; between F and O form virtual magnified images.
diagram_description: Table or diagram showing all object positions for convex lens with corresponding image positions, sizes, and natures
subject: physics

summary: Ray diagrams for convex lenses use convenient rays: one parallel to the axis refracts through the far focus, one through the near focus refracts parallel, and one through the optical center passes undeviated. Image is located where refracted rays intersect.
diagram_description: Ray diagram showing convex lens with multiple incident rays, their refraction paths, and image formation by ray intersection
subject: physics

summary: A concave lens always forms a virtual, erect, and diminished image on the same side of the lens as the object, regardless of object position. The image is located between the optical center and the focus. Concave lenses diverge all light rays.
diagram_description: Diagram showing concave lens with object on left side and virtual image appearing on the same side with rays illustrated
subject: physics

summary: For a concave lens, objects at infinity form point-sized virtual images at the focus. Objects at finite distances form virtual images between the focus and optical center. As the object moves away from the lens, the image becomes smaller and moves closer to the focus.
diagram_description: Table or diagram showing concave lens with different object positions and corresponding virtual image properties
subject: physics

summary: For concave lenses, a ray parallel to the axis appears to diverge from the near focus after refraction. A ray aimed at the far focus diverges parallel to the axis. A ray through the optical center passes undeviated. Virtual image is located where refracted rays appear to originate.
diagram_description: Ray diagram for concave lens showing parallel ray diverging from focus and virtual image formation on same side as object
subject: physics

subtopic_names: Speed of Light in Different Media
summary: Light generally travels in straight lines in a uniform medium, indicated by rays. Light from the sun enables us to see objects as they reflect light. Diffraction occurs when light passes very small obstacles. Light has wave-particle duality—it behaves as waves for diffraction and as particles for interaction with matter.
diagram_description: Diagram showing light ray traveling in straight line, object casting sharp shadow, and illustration of diffraction effect
subject: physics

summary: Light travels in straight lines in a uniform transparent medium, represented as rays. A sharp shadow cast by a small light source on an opaque object demonstrates this straight-line path. This principle forms the basis for ray optics.
diagram_description: Diagram showing point light source, opaque object, and sharp shadow demonstrating straight-line propagation
subject: physics

concept_name: Speed of Light in Different Media
summary: Light travels fastest in vacuum at 3 × 10⁸ m/s. Its speed is slightly less in air and reduces significantly in denser media like glass or water. The change in speed causes refraction at interfaces. The ratio of speeds in two media defines the relative refractive index.
diagram_description: Graph or table comparing speed of light in vacuum, air, water, and glass
subject: physics

summary: Light exhibits both wave and particle properties. It behaves as waves in phenomena like diffraction and interference. It behaves as particles (photons) in interactions with matter like photoelectric effect. Modern quantum theory reconciles both aspects—light is neither purely wave nor particle but has dual nature.
diagram_description: Diagram illustrating wave-particle duality showing diffraction pattern and photoelectric effect
subject: physics

summary: For lenses, sign conventions are similar to mirrors but with measurements from the optical center instead of pole. Convex lenses have positive focal length, concave lenses have negative focal length. Distances measured away from the lens are positive, toward it are negative.
diagram_description: Coordinate system diagram showing lens at origin with principal axis and sign convention for object and image distances
subject: physics

concept_name: Structure and Function of the Human Eye
summary: The human eye is a spherical optical instrument approximately 2.3 cm in diameter that forms inverted real images on the retina. Light enters through the cornea (which performs most refraction), passes through the crystalline lens, and is focused onto the light-sensitive retina. The iris controls pupil size to regulate light entry, while the optic nerve transmits signals to the brain.
diagram_description: Labeled cross-section of the human eye showing cornea, iris, pupil, crystalline lens, retina, optic nerve, aqueous and vitreous humours, and ciliary muscles.
subject: physics
chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

parent_concept_name: Structure and Function of the Human Eye
summary: The cornea is a transparent membrane forming the front bulge of the eye and performs most of the refraction for light entering the eye. The crystalline lens, a flexible jelly-like structure, provides fine adjustment of focal length to focus objects at different distances on the retina.
diagram_description: Schematic showing light path through cornea and lens with principal axis and focal region on retina.
subject: physics
chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

parent_concept_name: Structure and Function of the Human Eye
summary: The retina is a delicate light-sensitive membrane at the back of the eye containing millions of light-sensitive cells. These cells are activated by light and generate electrical signals transmitted via the optic nerve to the brain, which interprets them as images. The eye lens forms an inverted real image on the retina.
diagram_description: Diagram showing light-sensitive cells on retina converting photons to electrical signals sent through optic nerve.
subject: physics
chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

parent_concept_name: Structure and Function of the Human Eye
summary: The iris is a dark muscular diaphragm that controls the size of the pupil, which regulates the amount of light entering the eye. The pupil widens in dim light to allow more light entry and constricts in bright light to protect the retina.
diagram_description: Diagram showing iris structure and pupil dilation/constriction in different light conditions.
subject: physics
chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

parent_concept_name: Structure and Function of the Human Eye
summary: The ciliary muscles adjust the curvature of the crystalline lens to change its focal length, enabling the eye to focus on both near and distant objects. When muscles relax, the lens becomes thin (increased focal length) for distant vision; when they contract, the lens thickens (decreased focal length) for near vision. The near point (minimum comfortable distance) is ~25 cm for normal eyes, and the far point is infinity.
diagram_description: Diagram showing ciliary muscle contraction and lens shape changes for accommodation at near and far distances.
subject: physics
chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

concept_name: Refractive Defects of Vision and Correction
summary: Refractive defects occur when the eye cannot focus light properly on the retina due to changes in lens curvature or eyeball shape. The three main defects—myopia, hypermetropia, and presbyopia—can be corrected using appropriate spherical lenses or surgical interventions.
diagram_description: Comparison table or diagram showing the three refractive defects with images formed at different positions relative to retina.

builds_upon.concept_name: Structure and Function of the Human Eye
builds_upon.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

subject: physics
chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

parent_concept_name: Refractive Defects of Vision and Correction
summary: Myopia (near-sightedness) is a refractive defect where distant objects cannot be seen clearly because the image forms in front of the retina. It arises from excessive lens curvature or elongation of the eyeball. A concave lens of appropriate power diverges light rays to bring the focal point back onto the retina.
diagram_description: Ray diagram showing myopic eye with image formation in front of retina and correction using a concave (diverging) lens.
subject: physics
chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

parent_concept_name: Refractive Defects of Vision and Correction
summary: Hypermetropia (far-sightedness) is a refractive defect where nearby objects cannot be seen clearly because the image forms behind the retina. It results from a lens with excessive focal length or a shortened eyeball. A convex lens of suitable power converges light rays to shift the focal point onto the retina.
diagram_description: Ray diagram showing hypermetropic eye with image formation behind retina and correction using a convex (converging) lens.
subject: physics
chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

parent_concept_name: Refractive Defects of Vision and Correction
summary: Presbyopia is an age-related defect caused by weakening of ciliary muscles and reduced flexibility of the eye lens, making it difficult to focus on nearby objects. It typically develops in people over 40 and may coexist with myopia or hypermetropia. Bi-focal lenses (concave upper portion for distant vision, convex lower portion for near vision) are commonly used for correction.
diagram_description: Diagram of bi-focal lens showing concave and convex portions with ray paths for near and far vision correction.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

subject: physics
chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

summary: When light passes through a triangular prism with non-parallel refracting surfaces, it undergoes refraction at both surfaces and emerges deviated from its original direction. Different colours of light bend at different angles, causing white light to split into its component colours—a phenomenon called dispersion.
diagram_description: Ray diagram of light refraction through a prism showing incident ray, refracted ray, emergent ray, and angle of deviation.
subject: physics
chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

summary: Light refracted through a triangular glass prism bends toward the normal at the first surface (air to glass) and away from the normal at the second surface (glass to air). The emergent ray deviates from the incident ray by an angle called the angle of deviation, which depends on the angle of incidence and the prism's angle.
diagram_description: Labeled ray diagram of a triangular prism showing incident ray PE, refracted ray EF, emergent ray FS, and all relevant angles.
subject: physics
chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

summary: Dispersion is the splitting of white light into its seven component colours (VIBGYOR) when passing through a prism. Different colours bend at different angles because they have different wavelengths and travel at different speeds in the prism material—red light bends least, violet light bends most.
diagram_description: Diagram showing a white light beam entering a prism and exiting as a separated spectrum band with colours labeled in sequence.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

subject: physics
chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

summary: A spectrum is the band of separated colours produced when white light is dispersed. The colours appear in the sequence Violet, Indigo, Blue, Green, Yellow, Orange, and Red (VIBGYOR) based on increasing wavelength. Isaac Newton demonstrated that white light is a combination of these seven colours by recombining dispersed light with an inverted prism.
diagram_description: Diagram of the visible light spectrum showing the seven colours in sequence with approximate wavelength ranges.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

subject: physics
chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

summary: Natural optical phenomena result from interactions between sunlight and Earth's atmosphere or water droplets. These include rainbows (dispersion by water droplets), atmospheric refraction effects (twinkling stars, sunrise timing), and light scattering (blue sky, Tyndall effect).
diagram_description: Composite illustration showing a rainbow, twinkling star, and atmospheric scattering with light path annotations.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

subject: physics
chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

summary: A rainbow is a natural spectrum formed after rain when sunlight is refracted, internally reflected, and dispersed by millions of tiny water droplets in the atmosphere. It always appears opposite the Sun, with red light on the outer edge and violet on the inner edge. The sequence of colours results from differential bending of each colour by water droplets acting as small prisms.
diagram_description: Diagram showing sunlight entering a water droplet, undergoing refraction, internal reflection, and re-emergence with separated colours.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

subject: physics
chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

summary: Atmospheric refraction occurs as light from celestial objects passes through layers of air with varying density and refractive index. This causes apparent shifts in position of stars and planets, timing changes in sunrise/sunset, and the twinkling of stars due to turbulent air movement. The Sun appears about 2 minutes earlier at sunrise and 2 minutes later at sunset than the actual crossing of the horizon.
diagram_description: Diagram showing light path bending through atmospheric layers of increasing density with actual and apparent positions marked.
subject: physics
chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

summary: Scattering is the diffuse reflection of light by small particles (smoke, dust, water droplets, air molecules) in the atmosphere. The Tyndall effect becomes visible when a light beam passes through a colloidal suspension. The colour of scattered light depends on particle size: fine particles scatter blue (shorter wavelengths) more effectively, while larger particles scatter red (longer wavelengths).
diagram_description: Diagram showing light beam scattering by colloidal particles with different particle sizes affecting colour of scattered light.
subject: physics
chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

summary: The sky appears blue because air molecules and fine particles smaller than the wavelength of visible light scatter blue light (shorter wavelength, ~450 nm) much more strongly than red light (longer wavelength, ~700 nm). Red light is transmitted with minimal scattering, while the scattered blue light reaches the observer's eyes from all directions. At high altitudes where scattering is minimal, the sky appears dark.
diagram_description: Diagram showing atmospheric layers with sunlight scattering by molecules, showing preferential blue scattering and red transmission.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

subject: physics
chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

summary: Stars twinkle because starlight undergoes continuous refraction as it passes through Earth's atmosphere with varying density and refractive index. Since stars are essentially point sources, small changes in light path cause noticeable fluctuations in both apparent position and brightness—appearing alternately brighter and fainter. Planets do not twinkle because they are extended sources and variations in individual rays average out.
diagram_description: Diagram showing starlight bending through turbulent atmospheric layers with apparent position shifting, showing point vs extended source.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

subject: physics
chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

summary: Cataract is an age-related condition where the crystalline lens becomes milky and cloudy, causing partial or complete vision loss. It can be surgically corrected by removing the clouded lens. Not all eye donors have this defect; even people who have undergone cataract surgery can donate their eyes.
diagram_description: Cross-section of an eye showing a cloudy cataract lens compared to a normal clear lens.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

subject: physics
chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

summary: Bi-focal lenses contain two lens powers in one spectacle: the upper portion is concave for correcting myopia (distant vision), and the lower portion is convex for correcting hypermetropia (near vision). This design allows people with both defects simultaneously (common in older adults) to see clearly at all distances without changing glasses.
diagram_description: Diagram of a bi-focal lens showing concave upper section and convex lower section with ray paths for near and far vision.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

subject: physics
chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

summary: Beyond spectacles, refractive defects can be corrected using contact lenses or surgical interventions. These modern methods directly address the optical error by placing a corrective lens directly on the cornea or reshaping the corneal surface.

builds_upon.concept_name: Refractive Defects of Vision and Correction
builds_upon.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

subject: physics
chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

summary: Isaac Newton used two prisms to demonstrate that white light comprises seven colours: he dispersed sunlight through one prism, then recombined the separated colours using an inverted second prism to reform white light. This conclusively proved that colours are not added to light but are constituent parts of white light.
diagram_description: Diagram showing Newton's two-prism setup with white light entering first prism, spectrum exiting to second inverted prism, and white light re-emerging.

builds_upon.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

subject: physics
chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

summary: Covers: Electric Current, Electric Circuit, Ammeter.
subject: physics

summary: Electric current is the rate of flow of electric charges through a conductor, measured as charge passing per unit time. In metallic conductors, electrons carry the charge, but conventionally current direction is taken opposite to electron flow. Current is measured in amperes (A), where 1 A = 1 C/s.
diagram_description: Diagram showing electron flow (right to left) and conventional current direction (left to right) in a conductor with arrow markings.
subject: physics

summary: An electric circuit is a continuous and closed path through which electric current flows, consisting of a source (cell/battery), conductor wires, and electrical component(s). A switch makes or breaks the conducting link. If the circuit is broken anywhere, current stops flowing.
diagram_description: Schematic circuit diagram with cell, bulb, ammeter, wires, and plug key showing closed path.
subject: physics

summary: An ammeter is an instrument that measures electric current in a circuit. It is always connected in series with the circuit so that the full current flows through it. An ideal ammeter has negligible resistance.
diagram_description: Schematic symbol of ammeter (A in circle) and its series connection in a circuit diagram.
subject: physics

summary: Covers: Electric Potential Difference, Volt, Voltmeter.
subject: physics

summary: Electric potential difference between two points is the work done per unit charge to move the charge from one point to the other. It is the driving force that causes charges to flow in a conductor. The SI unit is volt (V), where 1 V = 1 J/C.
diagram_description: Diagram showing two points in a conductor with potential difference V between them, with work and charge labeled.
subject: physics

summary: The volt (V) is the SI unit of electric potential and potential difference, named after Alessandro Volta. One volt is the potential difference between two points when 1 joule of work is done to move 1 coulomb of charge.
subject: physics

summary: A voltmeter is an instrument that measures the potential difference between two points in a circuit. It is always connected in parallel across the two points whose potential difference is to be measured. An ideal voltmeter has very high resistance.
diagram_description: Schematic symbol of voltmeter (V in circle) and its parallel connection across two points in a circuit.
subject: physics

summary: Covers: Ohm's Law, Electrical Resistance, Resistivity, Variable Resistance.
subject: physics

summary: Ohm's Law states that the potential difference across the ends of a conductor is directly proportional to the current flowing through it, provided temperature remains constant. This relationship defines electrical resistance as the proportionality constant. The law is expressed as V = IR.
diagram_description: Graph of potential difference (V) versus current (I) showing linear relationship passing through origin for a ohmic conductor.
subject: physics

summary: Electrical resistance is a property of a conductor that opposes the flow of electric charge through it. The SI unit is the ohm (Ω), defined as the resistance when 1 A of current flows at 1 V potential difference. Resistance depends on the material, length, and cross-sectional area of the conductor.
diagram_description: Diagram showing a conductor with current flowing through it and internal opposition to current flow indicated by collision marks.
subject: physics

summary: Resistivity (ρ) is a characteristic property of a material that determines how strongly it opposes current flow, independent of the material's shape or size. The SI unit is ohm-meter (Ω m). Resistance of a conductor is related to resistivity by R = ρl/A, where l is length and A is cross-sectional area.
diagram_description: Table comparing resistivity values of conductors (metals, alloys) and insulators showing the wide range from 10⁻⁸ to 10¹⁷ Ω m.
subject: physics

summary: A variable resistance (rheostat) is a device used to regulate or change the current in an electric circuit without changing the voltage source. It allows adjustment of resistance by changing the length of the conducting wire in the circuit through a sliding contact.
diagram_description: Schematic symbol of a rheostat (resistor with arrow through it) and internal construction showing sliding contact on wire.
subject: physics

summary: Covers: Dependence on Length, Dependence on Cross-section, Dependence on Material.
subject: physics

summary: The resistance of a uniform conductor is directly proportional to its length. Doubling the length doubles the resistance, as the charge carriers encounter more collisions with atoms over a longer path.
diagram_description: Two wires of same material and cross-section but different lengths, with resistance values showing doubling of R when length doubles.
subject: physics

summary: The resistance of a uniform conductor is inversely proportional to its cross-sectional area. A thicker wire offers less resistance to current flow because charge carriers have more paths available to move through.
diagram_description: Two wires of same material and length but different diameters, with resistance values showing that thicker wire has lower resistance.
subject: physics

summary: The resistance of a conductor depends on the material from which it is made, characterized by its resistivity. Different materials have different atomic structures and free electron densities, resulting in vastly different resistivities—metals have low resistivity, insulators have very high resistivity.
diagram_description: Diagram showing three wires of same length and cross-section made of different materials (copper, nichrome, constantan) with different resistance values.
subject: physics

summary: Covers: Series Combination, Parallel Combination.
subject: physics

summary: In a series combination, resistors are connected end-to-end so the same current flows through each. The total potential difference equals the sum of individual potential differences, and the equivalent resistance equals the sum of individual resistances: Rs = R1 + R2 + R3 + ...
diagram_description: Circuit diagram showing three resistors connected in series with current flow arrows and potential difference markers across each resistor.
subject: physics

summary: In a parallel combination, resistors are connected between the same two points so each has the same potential difference. The total current divides among the branches, and the reciprocal of equivalent resistance equals the sum of reciprocals: 1/Rp = 1/R1 + 1/R2 + 1/R3 + ...
diagram_description: Circuit diagram showing three resistors connected in parallel with same potential difference across each and current dividing into three branches.
subject: physics

summary: Covers: Joule's Law of Heating, Electric Power, Practical Applications of Heating Effect.
subject: physics

summary: Joule's Law states that the heat produced in a resistor is directly proportional to the square of current, directly proportional to resistance, and directly proportional to time: H = I²Rt. This heat is generated because charge carriers collide with atoms, transferring their kinetic energy as thermal energy.
diagram_description: Graph showing heat produced (H) versus current (I) demonstrating quadratic relationship, or diagram of resistor glowing due to Joule heating.
subject: physics

summary: Electric power is the rate at which electrical energy is dissipated or consumed in a circuit, given by P = VI. The SI unit is the watt (W), where 1 W = 1 V × 1 A. Power can also be expressed as P = I²R or P = V²/R using Ohm's Law.
diagram_description: Diagram showing power consumption in a resistor with voltage and current labeled, illustrating P = VI relationship.
subject: physics

summary: The heating effect of electric current is used in many practical applications including electric heaters, toasters, irons, kettles, and light bulbs (using tungsten filament with high melting point). Fuses protect circuits by melting when excessive current flows, breaking the circuit before damage occurs.
diagram_description: Diagram showing cross-section of electric bulb with tungsten filament glowing, or fuse cartridge showing fuse wire encased in porcelain.
subject: physics

summary: Covers: Kilowatt-hour, Energy Consumption Calculations.
subject: physics

summary: The kilowatt-hour (kW h) is the commercial unit of electrical energy, commonly called a 'unit'. One kW h is the energy consumed when 1000 watts of power is used for 1 hour. 1 kW h = 3.6 × 10⁶ joules.
diagram_description: Diagram of an electricity meter showing kW h reading, or conversion showing 1 kW h = 1000 W × 3600 s.
subject: physics

summary: Electrical energy consumed by a device is calculated as the product of its power rating and the time of operation: E = Pt. When power is in kilowatts and time is in hours, energy is in kW h. The cost of energy is found by multiplying kW h by the per-unit rate.
diagram_description: Example calculation showing energy consumption of an appliance rated in watts over hours and its corresponding cost.
subject: physics

summary: Standard circuit symbols are conventional graphical representations used in circuit diagrams: cell (long and short lines), battery (multiple cells), open switch (open circle), closed switch (filled circle), resistor (zigzag or rectangle), ammeter (A in circle), voltmeter (V in circle), wire joint (filled dot), and crossing wires (arc without touching).
diagram_description: Table or diagram showing standard electrical circuit symbols including cell, battery, switch, resistor, ammeter, voltmeter, and wire configurations.
subject: physics

summary: Covers: Coulomb, Elementary Charge, Circuit Symbols and Diagrams, Standard Circuit Symbols.
subject: physics

summary: The coulomb (C) is the SI unit of electric charge. It is defined as the charge equivalent to 1 ampere of current flowing for 1 second (1 C = 1 A × 1 s). One coulomb is approximately the charge contained in 6 × 10¹⁸ electrons.
subject: physics

summary: The elementary charge is the magnitude of electric charge carried by a single electron, equal to 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ coulombs. One coulomb of charge corresponds to the charge of approximately 6 × 10¹⁸ electrons.
subject: physics

summary: An electric cell generates potential difference through chemical action, even when no current is drawn. A battery is a combination of multiple cells connected together. The chemical energy within a cell is converted to electrical energy to maintain current in a circuit.
diagram_description: Diagram showing internal structure of a cell with terminals and circuit symbol, or multiple cells arranged in series to form a battery.
subject: physics

summary: The watt (W) is the SI unit of power, defined as 1 joule of energy per second (1 W = 1 J/s). It is the power when 1 ampere of current flows at 1 volt potential difference. Kilowatt (kW) = 1000 W is commonly used for household appliances.
subject: physics

summary: The ampere (A) is the SI unit of electric current, named after André-Marie Ampère. One ampere is defined as the flow of 1 coulomb of charge per second (1 A = 1 C/s). Smaller currents are expressed in milliampere (mA = 10⁻³ A) or microampere (μA = 10⁻⁶ A).
subject: physics

summary: The ohm (Ω) is the SI unit of electrical resistance, named after Georg Simon Ohm. One ohm is the resistance of a conductor when 1 ampere of current flows at 1 volt potential difference (1 Ω = 1 V/1 A).
subject: physics

summary: Good conductors (metals like copper, silver) have very low resistivity (10⁻⁸ to 10⁻⁶ Ω m) due to abundance of free electrons, allowing easy current flow. Insulators (rubber, glass) have very high resistivity (10¹² to 10¹⁷ Ω m) with few free electrons, effectively blocking current flow.
diagram_description: Comparison table of resistivity values for good conductors, alloys, and insulators showing exponential difference.
subject: physics

summary: Alloys like nichrome, constantan, and manganin are preferred over pure metals for heating elements because they have higher resistivity (generating more heat for given current), higher melting points, and do not oxidize readily at high temperatures, making them ideal for electric irons, toasters, and heaters.
diagram_description: Table showing resistivity and melting points of common alloys used in heating devices compared to pure metals.
subject: physics

subtopic_names: Magnetic Field Strength and Current Relationship
summary: A magnetic field is the region surrounding a magnet where magnetic force can be detected. Magnetic field lines represent the path along which a north pole would move and emerge from the north pole to merge at the south pole. Field lines are closer together where the field is stronger.
diagram_description: Magnetic field lines radiating from a bar magnet with iron filings arrangement pattern
subject: physics

summary: The region surrounding a magnet in which the force of the magnet can be detected is called a magnetic field. Every magnet exerts influence in the space around it, detected by compass needles or iron filings.
diagram_description: Iron filings arranged in a pattern around a bar magnet showing field regions
subject: physics

summary: Magnetic field lines are imaginary curves that represent the path a free north pole would take in a magnetic field. By convention, field lines emerge from the north pole and merge at the south pole, forming closed curves inside and outside the magnet.
diagram_description: Pattern of magnetic field lines around a bar magnet with directional arrows
subject: physics

summary: Magnetic field lines form closed curves with no two lines crossing each other. Field line density indicates field strength—closer lines mean stronger field. The deflection of a compass needle increases as it moves toward the magnetic poles.
diagram_description: Comparison of field line density in regions of strong versus weak magnetic field
subject: physics

subtopic_names: Magnetic Field Around a Straight Conductor
Magnetic Field of a Circular Loop
summary: An electric current flowing through a conductor produces a magnetic field around it. The pattern and strength of this field depend on the shape of the conductor. Field strength increases with current and decreases with distance from the conductor.
diagram_description: Concentric circular field lines surrounding a current-carrying straight wire with direction indicated
subject: physics

concept_name: Magnetic Field Around a Straight Conductor
summary: A straight conductor carrying current produces concentric circular magnetic field lines centered on the wire. When current direction reverses, the field direction reverses. Field strength increases with current magnitude and decreases with distance from the wire.
diagram_description: Concentric circles of magnetic field around a vertical straight current-carrying wire with compass needle deflections
subject: physics

summary: To find the direction of a magnetic field around a straight current-carrying conductor, hold the conductor with your right thumb pointing in the current direction. Your fingers curl in the direction of the magnetic field lines, which form concentric circles around the wire.
diagram_description: Right hand demonstrating thumb pointing along current direction with fingers curling to show field direction

builds_upon.concept_name: Magnetic Field Around a Straight Conductor

subject: physics

concept_name: Magnetic Field of a Circular Loop
summary: A circular loop carrying current produces concentric arcs that appear as straight lines at the center. Every section of the wire contributes to the field in the same direction inside the loop, creating a uniform field pattern. With n turns, field strength is n times that of a single turn.
diagram_description: Magnetic field lines of a circular current-carrying coil showing straight field lines through the center
subject: physics

summary: A solenoid is a coil of insulated wire wrapped in a cylinder. Its magnetic field pattern resembles a bar magnet with north and south poles. Inside the solenoid, field lines are parallel and straight, indicating a uniform field throughout.
diagram_description: Field lines inside and around a solenoid showing uniform parallel lines similar to a bar magnet

builds_upon.concept_name: Magnetic Field of a Circular Loop

subject: physics

summary: An electromagnet is formed by wrapping insulated copper wire around a soft iron core and passing current through it. The strong uniform field inside the solenoid magnetizes the iron, creating a magnet whose strength depends on the current and number of turns.
diagram_description: Cross-section of a solenoid with iron core showing magnetic field magnetizing the iron material
subject: physics

summary: A current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field experiences a force perpendicular to both the current direction and field direction. Force magnitude is maximum when current and field are perpendicular. Force direction reverses when either current or field direction reverses.
diagram_description: Rod suspended between magnet poles showing displacement due to electromagnetic force
subject: physics

summary: The force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field depends on the current magnitude, field strength, and angle between them. Maximum force occurs at right angles. The conductor displaces in the direction of this force, proportional to current and field strength.
diagram_description: Aluminum rod between magnet poles showing leftward and rightward displacement for opposite current directions
subject: physics

summary: To find the direction of force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field, hold your left hand with thumb, forefinger, and middle finger mutually perpendicular. The forefinger points in the field direction, the middle finger points in the current direction, and the thumb points in the force direction.
diagram_description: Left hand with three fingers extended showing field, current, and force directions
subject: physics

summary: A domestic electric circuit receives 220 V AC power through live (red) and neutral (black) wires. The earth wire (green) provides safety grounding. Separate circuits with 15 A and 5 A ratings serve high and low power appliances respectively. Fuses protect against overcurrent.
diagram_description: Schematic diagram of household electrical distribution with fuses, switches, and multiple appliances
subject: physics

summary: A domestic circuit consists of a live wire (red, 220 V), neutral wire (black, reference), and earth wire (green, safety ground). The electricity meter measures consumption, the main switch controls supply, and separate circuits branch to high and low power appliances through the distribution box.
diagram_description: Schematic showing path of live and neutral wires from meter through main switch to distribution box and appliances
subject: physics

summary: A fuse prevents circuit damage by melting when current exceeds its rating due to short-circuiting or overloading. Short-circuiting occurs when live and neutral wires directly contact or appliances are faulty. Joule heating in the fuse melts it to break the circuit before damage occurs.
diagram_description: Fuse element melting when excessive current flows through it
subject: physics

summary: The earth wire (green insulation) connects metallic appliance bodies to a metal plate buried in earth, providing a safe low-resistance path for leakage current. If current leaks to a metallic body, it flows harmlessly to earth rather than through the user, preventing electric shock.
diagram_description: Grounding path from metallic appliance through earth wire to buried earth plate
subject: physics

concept_name: Hans Christian Oersted and Electromagnetism Discovery
summary: Hans Christian Oersted (1777–1851) discovered in 1820 that a compass needle deflects when an electric current passes through a nearby metallic wire. This observation proved the fundamental connection between electricity and magnetism. The unit oersted honors his contribution to electromagnetic science.
diagram_description: Historical apparatus showing compass needle deflecting due to current through wire
subject: physics

concept_name: Magnetic Field Strength and Current Relationship
summary: The magnitude of a magnetic field produced by a current-carrying conductor is directly proportional to the current and inversely proportional to the distance from the conductor. Increasing current increases field strength; moving away from the wire decreases field strength.
diagram_description: Graph showing magnetic field strength versus current and distance from conductor

builds_upon.concept_name: Magnetic Field Around a Straight Conductor

subject: physics

summary: A compass needle is a small bar magnet that aligns with magnetic field direction. Its north-seeking pole points toward a field's north direction and south-seeking pole toward south. Deflection angle and magnitude indicate field strength at that location.
diagram_description: Compass needle aligned with magnetic field lines at various positions around a current-carrying wire
subject: physics

summary: Physics is a quantitative science requiring comparison of physical quantities with standardized reference units. Base quantities (length, mass, time, etc.) have corresponding base units, while derived quantities are expressed as combinations of base units. A complete set of units is called a system of units.
subject: physics

summary: Fundamental (base) quantities are independently defined physical properties like length, mass, and time. Derived quantities are expressed as mathematical combinations of base quantities, such as velocity (length/time) and force (mass × length/time²).
subject: physics

summary: Earlier unit systems included CGS (centimetre, gram, second), FPS (foot, pound, second), and MKS (metre, kilogram, second). These were largely replaced by the internationally standardized SI system due to its decimal basis and convenience.
subject: physics

summary: The SI system defines seven base units: metre (length), kilogram (mass), second (time), ampere (electric current), kelvin (temperature), mole (amount of substance), and candela (luminous intensity). SI is the internationally accepted standard established by BIPM and uses decimal conversions for simplicity.
diagram_description: Table listing the seven SI base quantities, their unit names, symbols, and physical definitions
subject: physics

summary: Dimensional analysis expresses physical quantities in terms of base dimensions [L], [M], [T], [A], [K], [mol], [cd]. It enables checking equation correctness through the principle of homogeneity (all terms must have identical dimensions) and deducing relationships among physical quantities.
subject: physics

summary: Dimensions describe the nature of a physical quantity as powers of base quantities. For example, volume has dimensions [L³], velocity has [LT⁻¹], and force has [MLT⁻²]. Dimensions are independent of the unit system used.
subject: physics

summary: A dimensional formula shows how base quantities combine to represent a physical quantity, written as [M^a L^b T^c...]. A dimensional equation equates a physical quantity to its dimensional formula, e.g., [Force] = [MLT⁻²].
subject: physics

summary: An equation is dimensionally consistent if all terms on both sides have identical dimensions. This principle checks equation correctness without committing to specific units. A dimensionally incorrect equation is definitely wrong, but dimensional correctness alone does not guarantee physical accuracy.
subject: physics

summary: Physical relations can be deduced by assuming a power-law dependence and matching dimensions on both sides. For example, assuming T ∝ l^x g^y m^z and equating exponents yields the period formula. This method finds functional forms but cannot determine dimensionless constants.
subject: physics

summary: Significant figures represent the precision of measurement: all certain digits plus the first uncertain digit. Rules govern counting (non-zero digits always count; zeros between non-zero digits count; leading zeros don't count), and arithmetic operations must respect the precision of input data.
subject: physics

summary: Significant figures are the reliable digits in a measurement plus the first uncertain digit. They indicate measurement precision, which depends on the least count of the instrument. The number of significant figures is independent of unit choice.
subject: physics

summary: Rules: all non-zero digits count; zeros between non-zero digits count; leading zeros don't count; trailing zeros in a decimal number count; trailing zeros without decimal don't count. Scientific notation eliminates ambiguity by showing all base-number zeros as significant.
subject: physics

summary: In multiplication/division, the result retains as many significant figures as the least-precise input. In addition/subtraction, the result retains as many decimal places as the least-precise input. These rules ensure final results reflect input measurement precision.
subject: physics

summary: Raise the preceding digit by 1 if the dropped digit is >5; leave unchanged if <5. If the dropped digit equals 5, round to the nearest even digit. In multi-step calculations, retain one extra digit in intermediate steps to avoid accumulating rounding errors.
subject: physics

summary: Measurement uncertainty depends on instrument precision and the measured value. Relative error is the ratio of uncertainty to the value (expressed as percentage). When combining measurements, errors propagate differently in addition/subtraction versus multiplication/division.
subject: physics

concept_name: Scientific Notation and Order of Magnitude
summary: Scientific notation expresses numbers as a × 10^b where 1 ≤ a < 10. Order of magnitude is the exponent b when a is rounded to the nearest power of 10, providing a quick size estimate. This notation eliminates significant-figure ambiguity and simplifies large/small number handling.
subject: physics

parent_concept_name: Scientific Notation and Order of Magnitude
summary: Every number is expressed as a × 10^b with 1 ≤ a < 10 and b any integer. This standardized form clarifies significant figures: all zeros in the mantissa a are significant, and unit changes affect only the exponent.

builds_upon.concept_name: Scientific Notation and Order of Magnitude

subject: physics

parent_concept_name: Scientific Notation and Order of Magnitude
summary: Order of magnitude is the exponent b in the approximate form 10^b obtained by rounding a in a × 10^b. It provides a quick rough estimate of a quantity's scale. For example, Earth's diameter (~1.28 × 10^7 m) has order of magnitude 7.
subject: physics

summary: Plane angle dθ is the ratio of arc length ds to radius r (unit: radian, rad). Solid angle dΩ is the ratio of intercepted spherical area dA to radius squared r² (unit: steradian, sr). Both are dimensionless derived units defined alongside SI base units.
diagram_description: Geometric diagram showing a sector with arc length ds, radius r, and angle dθ; and a cone with apex O and solid angle dΩ
subject: physics

summary: Plane angle dθ is defined as the ratio of arc length ds to radius r: dθ = ds/r. The unit is radian (rad), a dimensionless quantity. It is the standard angular measure in physics.
diagram_description: Circular sector showing radius r, arc length ds, and angle dθ at the center
subject: physics

summary: Solid angle dΩ is the ratio of intercepted spherical surface area dA to the square of radius r: dΩ = dA/r². The unit is steradian (sr), a dimensionless derived unit analogous to radian for three-dimensional angles.
diagram_description: Cone with apex O showing spherical cap area dA and solid angle dΩ
subject: physics

subtopic_names: SI Prefixes for Multiples and Submultiples
summary: Units are converted by applying conversion factors, treating units algebraically. SI prefixes (like kilo-, centi-, milli-) scale units by powers of 10, enabling convenient expression of very large or very small quantities without altering significant figures.
diagram_description: Table of SI prefixes with their symbols, names, and corresponding powers of 10
subject: physics

summary: Units are converted by multiplying by conversion factors, treating units as algebraic symbols that can cancel. For example, 18 km h⁻¹ = 18 × (1000 m)/(3600 s) = 5 m s⁻¹. This method ensures correctness and prevents unit errors.
subject: physics

concept_name: SI Prefixes for Multiples and Submultiples
summary: SI prefixes scale units by powers of 10: kilo (10³), mega (10⁶), micro (10⁻⁶), nano (10⁻⁹), etc. They provide convenient notation for very large or small quantities while maintaining the same unit base, without changing the number of significant figures.
subject: physics

summary: Measurement precision depends on the least count (smallest division) of an instrument. Different instruments offer different precision levels: vernier calipers (~0.01 mm), screw gauge (~0.01 mm with 100 divisions), and optical instruments (wavelength precision). Choosing the appropriate instrument ensures reliable measurements.
subject: physics

summary: The least count is the smallest division on an instrument, determining measurement precision. A smaller least count indicates higher precision. Measurement precision directly reflects the least count: a metre scale (1 mm least count) is less precise than a screw gauge (0.01 mm).
subject: physics

summary: Select instruments based on required precision and measurement type. For length ~0.1 mm: use vernier calipers; for ~0.01 mm: use screw gauge with 100 divisions; for atomic-scale precision: use optical techniques. The chosen instrument must match the desired measurement accuracy.
subject: physics

summary: Indirect measurement techniques extend instrument capability: wrapping a thread around an object and measuring with a scale can estimate diameter; using a microscope with magnification allows observation of very small objects. Multiple measurements reduce random error and improve reliability.
subject: physics

summary: Some derived SI units have special names (joule, newton, watt, pascal) that simplify notation. Certain non-SI units are retained for general use: minute, hour, day (time); degree, litre, tonne (other). These are officially recognized but outside the formal SI system.
diagram_description: Table listing non-SI units retained for general use with their symbols and SI equivalents
subject: physics

summary: Motion is the change in position of an object with time. Position is specified relative to a chosen origin, with rightward taken as positive and leftward as negative in one dimension. Rectilinear motion refers to motion along a straight line, and objects are treated as point objects when their size is negligible compared to distances traveled.
diagram_description: Coordinate system with origin O, showing positive and negative directions along x-axis with object positions marked
subject: physics

summary: Motion is defined as the change in position of an object with respect to time. Every object in motion requires a reference frame or origin to describe its position changes.
subject: physics

summary: Position specifies an object's location relative to a chosen origin; displacement is the change in position from initial to final location. Displacement is a vector quantity and can be positive, negative, or zero. Distance is the total path length traveled, always positive.
diagram_description: x-t graph showing initial position x₁, final position x₂, and displacement Δx marked on the horizontal axis
subject: physics

summary: Rectilinear motion is motion confined to a straight line. It is the simplest form of motion and is described using a single coordinate axis. All motion in this chapter is analyzed as rectilinear motion.
subject: physics

summary: Velocity is the rate of change of position with time and is a vector quantity with direction. Speed is the magnitude of velocity. Average quantities describe motion over a time interval, while instantaneous quantities describe motion at a specific instant.
diagram_description: Position-time graph with secant line between two points showing average velocity, and tangent line at a point showing instantaneous velocity
subject: physics

summary: Average velocity is total displacement divided by total time interval; average speed is total distance divided by total time. Average speed is always greater than or equal to the magnitude of average velocity. Both describe motion over a finite time interval, not at a specific instant.
subject: physics

summary: Instantaneous velocity is the limit of average velocity as the time interval approaches zero; it equals the slope of the tangent to the position-time graph at that instant. Instantaneous speed is the magnitude of instantaneous velocity. At any instant, instantaneous speed always equals the magnitude of instantaneous velocity.
diagram_description: Position-time curve with tangent line at a specific point, slope of tangent representing instantaneous velocity
subject: physics

summary: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity with time, defined as a vector quantity. It describes how quickly velocity changes, resulting from changes in speed, direction, or both. Acceleration can be positive, negative (deceleration), or zero.
diagram_description: Velocity-time graph showing positive slope (positive acceleration), negative slope (negative acceleration), and zero slope (no acceleration)
subject: physics

summary: Average acceleration is the change in velocity divided by the time interval during which the change occurs. It represents the average rate of velocity change over a finite time period and equals the slope of the line connecting two points on a velocity-time graph.
subject: physics

summary: Instantaneous acceleration is the limit of average acceleration as the time interval approaches zero, equal to the slope of the tangent to the velocity-time graph at that instant. It describes the rate of velocity change at a specific moment in time.
diagram_description: Velocity-time graph with tangent line at a point, showing slope as instantaneous acceleration
subject: physics

summary: When acceleration is constant, average acceleration equals the instantaneous acceleration at any time. The position-time graph is parabolic, and the velocity-time graph is a straight line. This condition allows use of simple kinematic equations relating displacement, velocity, acceleration, and time.
diagram_description: Velocity-time graph showing linear relationship between velocity and time for constant acceleration
subject: physics

summary: Kinematic equations relate five quantities—displacement, initial velocity, final velocity, acceleration, and time—for uniformly accelerated motion. These three independent equations allow solving problems when three of five quantities are known. They apply only when acceleration is constant.
diagram_description: Velocity-time graph with shaded area under curve representing displacement for constant acceleration
subject: physics

summary: The velocity-time relation expresses final velocity as the sum of initial velocity and the product of acceleration and time. Graphically, this is a straight line on a velocity-time graph with slope equal to acceleration.
subject: physics

summary: The displacement-time relation expresses displacement as initial velocity times time plus half the product of acceleration and time squared. Graphically, the position-time curve is parabolic for constant non-zero acceleration.
subject: physics

summary: The velocity-displacement relation connects final velocity, initial velocity, acceleration, and displacement without explicit time dependence. This equation is especially useful when time is not known or required.
subject: physics

summary: Graphs provide visual representations of motion. Position-time graphs show displacement over time; the slope at any point gives instantaneous velocity. Velocity-time graphs show velocity changes; the slope gives acceleration, and the area under the curve equals displacement.
diagram_description: Three subplots showing: (1) x-t graph with positive acceleration (upward curve), (2) v-t graph with positive slope, (3) area under v-t curve
subject: physics

summary: Position-time graphs display position on the vertical axis and time on the horizontal axis. The slope of the graph at any point equals the instantaneous velocity. A straight line indicates constant velocity; a parabola indicates constant acceleration.
diagram_description: x-t graph showing curved (parabolic) path for accelerated motion with tangent line indicating instantaneous velocity
subject: physics

summary: Velocity-time graphs display velocity on the vertical axis and time on the horizontal axis. The slope of the graph equals instantaneous acceleration. A straight line indicates constant acceleration; the area under the curve equals displacement.
diagram_description: v-t graph showing linear relationship with positive slope (positive acceleration) and shaded area representing displacement
subject: physics

summary: The area enclosed between the velocity-time curve and the time axis equals the total displacement of the object during that time interval. For constant velocity, the area is rectangular; for constant acceleration, the area is trapezoidal.
diagram_description: v-t graph with shaded area under the line representing displacement, showing rectangular area for constant velocity and trapezoidal area for constant acceleration
subject: physics

summary: Free fall is motion of an object under the influence of gravity alone, with air resistance neglected. All objects fall with the same constant acceleration g = 9.8 m/s² near Earth's surface. Free fall is uniformly accelerated motion, so kinematic equations apply directly.
diagram_description: Diagram of object falling from rest with position marked at equal time intervals, showing increasing distances (Galileo's law of odd numbers)
subject: physics

summary: Acceleration due to gravity (g) is the constant acceleration experienced by any object in free fall near Earth's surface, with magnitude 9.8 m/s² (or approximately 10 m/s²). This acceleration is directed vertically downward and is independent of the object's mass or initial velocity.
subject: physics

summary: For free fall with initial velocity zero and upward direction positive, the kinematic equations become v = -gt, y = -½gt², and v² = -2gy. When initial velocity is upward, use the general kinematic equations with a = -g. The minus sign indicates downward acceleration.
subject: physics

summary: Kinematic equations apply to diverse real-world situations including free fall, projectile motion, and vehicle braking. These applications demonstrate how constant acceleration governs phenomena from distance ratios in falling bodies to braking distances proportional to the square of initial velocity.
subject: physics

summary: Galileo's law states that distances traversed by a freely falling body during successive equal time intervals are in the ratio of odd numbers 1:3:5:7:9:..., independent of the gravitational acceleration value. This elegant result emerges from the kinematic equation y = ½gt².
diagram_description: Diagram showing falling object at equal time intervals with distances marked in ratios 1:3:5:7:9
subject: physics

summary: Stopping distance—the distance traveled before a vehicle stops—is proportional to the square of initial velocity and inversely proportional to braking deceleration. Doubling initial velocity increases stopping distance by a factor of four. This relation is crucial for road safety and speed limit design.
diagram_description: Graph showing stopping distance vs. initial velocity as a parabolic curve, illustrating quadratic relationship
subject: physics

summary: Reaction time is the time interval between perception of a stimulus and initiation of response. It can be measured using a dropped ruler experiment, where the distance fallen under free fall determines the reaction time. Typical reaction time is 0.15–0.3 seconds for visual stimuli.
diagram_description: Diagram of ruler drop experiment between thumb and forefinger, with distance d marked
subject: physics

summary: Relative velocity is the velocity of an object as observed from a specific reference frame (usually another moving object). It depends on the choice of reference frame; velocities of different observers are related by vector addition.
