Relative velocity explains phenomena like a passenger appearing stationary relative to a train but moving relative to the ground.
diagram_description: Diagram showing two reference frames (ground and moving observer) with vectors indicating absolute and relative velocities
subject: physics

summary: Relative velocity of object A with respect to observer B is defined as the vector difference of their velocities: v_AB = v_A - v_B. It represents how fast A appears to be moving from B's perspective. The choice of reference frame is arbitrary and affects all velocity measurements.
subject: physics

summary: For one-dimensional motion, relative velocity calculations use algebraic subtraction with proper sign conventions. When two objects move along the same straight line, their relative velocity is simply the difference of their velocities along that line, accounting for direction.
subject: physics

summary: Kinematics is the branch of mechanics that describes how objects move without analyzing the forces causing motion. It focuses on relationships between displacement, velocity, acceleration, and time. The study of causes of motion (forces) is reserved for dynamics in subsequent chapters.
subject: physics

summary: Kinematics is the study of motion without considering the causes (forces) that produce motion. It develops the mathematical framework and concepts needed to describe and analyze motion of objects.
subject: physics

summary: Objects are treated as point objects (particles) when their size is much smaller than the distances they travel in reasonable time intervals. This approximation is valid for most macroscopic situations and simplifies analysis by ignoring rotational motion and internal structure.
subject: physics

summary: Scalars have magnitude only (distance, mass, temperature), while vectors have both magnitude and direction (displacement, velocity, acceleration). Vectors obey triangle and parallelogram laws of addition. Two vectors are equal only if they have identical magnitude and direction. A null vector has zero magnitude and results from adding equal and opposite vectors.
diagram_description: Side-by-side comparison of scalar (single number) vs vector (arrow showing magnitude and direction)
subject: physics

summary: A scalar is a physical quantity completely specified by a single number (magnitude) and proper unit. Scalars follow ordinary algebraic rules and can be added, subtracted, multiplied, or divided like normal numbers. Examples include distance, speed, mass, temperature, and time.
subject: physics

summary: A vector quantity has both magnitude and direction, obeying triangle or parallelogram laws of addition. Represented by bold letters (A) or arrows over letters (→A). Displacement, velocity, acceleration, and force are common vector quantities in mechanics.
diagram_description: Arrow representation of a vector showing magnitude (length) and direction (arrow orientation)
subject: physics

summary: Two vectors A and B are equal if and only if they have the same magnitude and same direction. Position is irrelevant—vectors can be shifted parallel to themselves without changing equality. Free vectors lack fixed location; local vectors have defined line of application.
diagram_description: Two equal vectors shown at different positions with matching arrows; comparison with unequal vectors of same magnitude but different directions
subject: physics

summary: A null vector has zero magnitude and undefined direction. Results from A + (−A) = 0 or multiplying any vector by zero (0A = 0). Also represents displacement when an object returns to its starting point.
subject: physics

summary: Vectors can be combined through algebraic operations: multiplication by scalars changes magnitude/direction; addition uses triangle or parallelogram methods; subtraction is defined as A − B = A + (−B). Vectors can be resolved into components along chosen directions or unit vectors.
diagram_description: Flow diagram showing vector operations: scalar multiplication, addition (head-to-tail and parallelogram), subtraction, and resolution into components
subject: physics

summary: Multiplying vector A by positive scalar λ gives λA with magnitude λ|A| in same direction. Multiplying by negative −λ gives magnitude λ|A| in opposite direction. Scalar can have physical dimensions; result carries combined dimensions.
diagram_description: Vector A with resulting vectors 2A and −1.5A showing changes in magnitude and direction
subject: physics

summary: Triangle method: place vector B tail-to-head with A; resultant R connects tail of A to head of B. Parallelogram method: place A and B at common origin; diagonal is R. Both obey commutative (A+B=B+A) and associative [(A+B)+C=A+(B+C)] laws.
diagram_description: Two diagrams showing triangle method (head-to-tail) and parallelogram method (common origin) producing identical resultant vector
subject: physics

summary: Vector subtraction A − B is defined as A + (−B), where −B is the opposite of B with same magnitude but reversed direction. Geometrically, place −B tail-to-head with A, or use parallelogram with −B.
diagram_description: Vector A with −B shown as reversed vector; resultant R₂ = A − B compared with R₁ = A + B
subject: physics

summary: A vector A in a plane can be resolved along two non-collinear directions a and b: A = λa + μb where λ, μ are scalars. Perpendicular directions (x, y axes) are most convenient, using unit vectors î and ĵ. Components can be positive, negative, or zero.
diagram_description: Vector A resolved into component vectors along two directions a and b using the parallelogram method
subject: physics

summary: Unit vectors have magnitude 1 and specify direction only. Standard Cartesian unit vectors: î (x-axis), ĵ (y-axis), k̂ (z-axis). Mutually perpendicular; any vector A can be written as A = Aₓî + Aᵧĵ + Aᵤk̂.
diagram_description: Orthogonal coordinate system showing î, ĵ, k̂ along x, y, z axes with |î| = |ĵ| = |k̂| = 1
subject: physics

summary: Any vector A in a plane can be expressed as A = Aₓî + Aᵧĵ where Aₓ and Aᵧ are x- and y-components. Magnitude: |A| = √(Aₓ² + Aᵧ²). If A makes angle θ with x-axis: Aₓ = A cos θ, Aᵧ = A sin θ.
diagram_description: Vector A in x-y plane showing components Aₓ and Aᵧ, with angle θ from x-axis and trigonometric relationships
subject: physics

summary: Cartesian components are projections of a vector onto x-, y-, and z-axes. For 2D: A = Aₓî + Aᵧĵ; for 3D: A = Aₓî + Aᵧĵ + Aᵤk̂. Components can be positive, negative, or zero depending on vector orientation.
diagram_description: 3D coordinate system showing vector A and its projections Aₓ, Aᵧ, Aᵤ onto the three axes
subject: physics

summary: If vector A makes angle θ with x-axis, components are found using trigonometry: Aₓ = A cos θ, Aᵧ = A sin θ. Conversely, given components: θ = tan⁻¹(Aᵧ/Aₓ). Angle can range from 0° to 360° or −180° to +180°.
diagram_description: Right triangle formed by vector A with components Aₓ, Aᵧ and angle θ from x-axis, with trig relationships
subject: physics

summary: To add vectors analytically: R = A + B, find Rₓ = Aₓ + Bₓ and Rᵧ = Aᵧ + Bᵧ. Each component of resultant equals sum of corresponding components. More accurate and efficient than graphical method. Extends to any number of vectors.
diagram_description: Two vectors A and B shown in component form with Rₓ and Rᵧ labeled as component sums
subject: physics

summary: Describes object motion in x-y plane using vectors. Position r = xî + yĵ; displacement Δr = r′ − r. Velocity v = dr/dt is tangent to path. Acceleration a = dv/dt changes both magnitude and direction. Motion separates into independent x and y components.
diagram_description: Curved path in x-y plane showing position vectors r and r′, displacement Δr, and velocity vector tangent to path
subject: physics

summary: Position vector r = xî + yĵ locates object relative to origin at time t. Displacement from P to P′ is Δr = r′ − r = Δxî + Δyĵ, independent of path taken. Displacement magnitude ≤ path length; equality only if no direction change.
diagram_description: x-y coordinate system with origin O, position vectors r and r′ to points P and P′, and displacement vector Δr
subject: physics

summary: Average velocity v̄ = Δr/Δt = (Δx/Δt)î + (Δy/Δt)ĵ = v̄ₓî + v̄ᵧĵ. Instantaneous velocity v = dr/dt is the limit as Δt → 0, always tangent to path. Components: vₓ = dx/dt, vᵧ = dy/dt.
diagram_description: Curved path showing shrinking displacement vectors as Δt decreases; final velocity vector tangent to path
subject: physics

summary: Average acceleration ā = Δv/Δt with components āₓ = Δvₓ/Δt, āᵧ = Δvᵧ/Δt. Instantaneous acceleration a = dv/dt = aₓî + aᵧĵ where aₓ = dvₓ/dt, aᵧ = dvᵧ/dt. Direction changes continuously; not necessarily tangent or radial.
diagram_description: Path with velocity vectors at successive points; velocity change vectors ΔV shown separately; acceleration vector
subject: physics

summary: When acceleration a is constant: v = v₀ + at and r = r₀ + v₀t + ½at². In components: vₓ = v₀ₓ + aₓt, x = x₀ + v₀ₓt + ½aₓt². Motion in perpendicular directions are independent; each obeys one-dimensional kinematics.
diagram_description: Parabolic trajectory in x-y plane showing initial position, velocity components, and constant acceleration vector
subject: physics

subtopic_names: Maximum Height and Time of Flight
summary: Projectile motion combines constant horizontal velocity (vₓ = v₀ cos θ₀) and constant downward acceleration (aᵧ = −g). Path is parabolic. Horizontal and vertical components are independent: x = (v₀ cos θ₀)t, y = (v₀ sin θ₀)t − ½gt². Used to analyze thrown/launched objects.
diagram_description: Projectile trajectory showing initial velocity at angle θ₀, horizontal and vertical components at various points, and parabolic path
subject: physics

summary: Position: x = (v₀ cos θ₀)t, y = (v₀ sin θ₀)t − ½gt². Velocity: vₓ = v₀ cos θ₀ (constant), vᵧ = v₀ sin θ₀ − gt. At maximum height, vᵧ = 0. Assumes no air resistance and level launch height.
diagram_description: Trajectory showing position and velocity vectors at launch, mid-flight, and landing; constant vₓ and varying vᵧ
subject: physics

summary: Eliminating time t from position equations gives: y = (tan θ₀)x − [gx²]/[2(v₀ cos θ₀)²]. This is parabolic (y = ax + bx²) with constants a, b depending on v₀ and θ₀. Shape independent of horizontal position; same for all launch angles.
diagram_description: Parabolic curve in x-y plane showing trajectory for a specific launch angle and speed; labeled with initial angle θ₀
subject: physics

concept_name: Maximum Height and Time of Flight
summary: At max height, vᵧ = 0; solving v₀ sin θ₀ − gtₘ = 0 gives tₘ = (v₀ sin θ₀)/g. Max height: hₘ = (v₀ sin θ₀)²/(2g). Total flight time (return to launch height): Tₓ = 2(v₀ sin θ₀)/g = 2tₘ. Symmetric trajectory.
diagram_description: Parabolic trajectory with maximum height hₘ marked, time tₘ to reach it, and total time Tₓ to return marked on curve
subject: physics

summary: Horizontal distance when projectile returns to launch height: R = (v₀² sin 2θ₀)/g. Maximum range Rₘ = v₀²/g occurs at θ₀ = 45°. For complementary angles (45° ± α), ranges are equal. Assumes level ground and no air resistance.
diagram_description: Two trajectories for launch angles 30° and 60° showing equal ranges; third trajectory at 45° showing maximum range
subject: physics

summary: Object moves at constant speed v along circular path of radius R. Speed is uniform but velocity direction changes continuously—requires centripetal acceleration aᶜ = v²/R always directed toward center. No tangential acceleration; only centripetal. Kinematics equations for constant acceleration do not apply.
diagram_description: Circular path with object at point P; velocity tangent to circle; position and velocity vectors at different points; acceleration toward center
subject: physics

summary: Centripetal acceleration aᶜ = v²/R is directed toward circle center, perpendicular to velocity. Magnitude is constant but direction changes. Can also be expressed as aᶜ = ω²R where ω is angular speed. Derived from similar triangles: Δv/v = Δr/R.
diagram_description: Circular path with velocity vectors at two points forming a triangle; change in velocity ΔV shown; similar triangles illustrating acceleration formula
subject: physics

summary: Angular speed ω = Δθ/Δt is rate of change of angular position (in rad/s). Related to linear speed by v = ωR. As angle Δθ subtended by arc changes, arc length Δs = RΔθ, so v = (Δs/Δt) = R(Δθ/Δt) = Rω.
diagram_description: Circular path with radius R; angle Δθ subtended at center; arc length Δs; showing relationship between angular and linear quantities
subject: physics

summary: Time period T is time for one complete revolution. Frequency ν = 1/T is number of revolutions per second (Hz). Related to angular speed: ω = 2πν = 2π/T. Linear speed: v = 2πRν = 2πR/T.
diagram_description: Clock face or circle showing one complete revolution in time T; marking frequency as revolutions per second
subject: physics

summary: Law of Cosines: for resultant R of vectors A and B with angle θ between them, R² = A² + B² + 2AB cos θ. Law of Sines: R/sin θ = A/sin β = B/sin α relates magnitudes and angles of vectors and resultant. Both facilitate analytical vector addition.
diagram_description: Vector triangle showing A, B, resultant R, and angles; Law of Cosines and Law of Sines labeled
subject: physics

summary: For two vectors A and B at angle θ, resultant magnitude R = √(A² + B² + 2AB cos θ). Derives from geometry of parallelogram: perpendicular components give right triangle. Special case: θ = 90° → R = √(A² + B²).
diagram_description: Parallelogram formed by vectors A and B; diagonal is resultant R; right triangle showing perpendicular height from B to A extended
subject: physics

summary: For vector triangle with sides A, B, R and opposite angles α, β, θ: R/sin θ = A/sin β = B/sin α. Allows finding direction (angle) of resultant given magnitudes. Combined with Law of Cosines for complete vector addition solution.
diagram_description: Vector triangle with all three sides (A, B, R) and angles (α, β, θ) labeled; showing proportionality of sides to sines of opposite angles
subject: physics

summary: Velocity is time derivative of position: v = dr/dt; acceleration is time derivative of velocity: a = dv/dt = d²r/dt². These definitions establish the causal chain: position → velocity → acceleration. In components, each relationship applies independently to x, y, z coordinates.
diagram_description: Flow diagram showing r(t) → differentiate → v(t) → differentiate → a(t); reverse: integrate a → v, integrate v → r
subject: physics

summary: Velocity of object relative to frame 1 = velocity relative to frame 2 + velocity of frame 2 relative to frame 1. In vector form: v₁₂ = v₁ − v₂. Both objects' velocities measured from same reference frame. Essential for analyzing motion in different coordinate systems.
diagram_description: Two reference frames with object; velocity vectors from each frame shown; vector subtraction illustrating relative velocity
subject: physics

summary: Projectile path is parabola y = (tan θ₀)x − gx²/[2(v₀ cos θ₀)²]. Vertex (max height) at xₘ = (v₀² sin 2θ₀)/(2g), symmetrical about vertical line. Focus and directrix properties of parabola relate to energy conservation and optimal trajectories.
diagram_description: Parabolic trajectory with vertex marked at maximum height; axes labeled with range and max height; focus and directrix shown
subject: physics

concept_name: Independence of Horizontal and Vertical Motion
summary: In projectile motion, horizontal motion (constant velocity) and vertical motion (constant acceleration) are independent. Horizontal displacement unaffected by vertical motion, and vice versa. This principle, identified by Galileo, allows 2D motion to be analyzed as two 1D problems.
diagram_description: Projectile trajectory with horizontal displacement marked separately from vertical displacement; two independent 1D motions side-by-side
subject: physics

summary: In uniform circular motion, velocity is always tangent to the circular path (perpendicular to radius). Acceleration (centripetal) is radial (toward center), perpendicular to velocity. The two are mutually perpendicular, unlike motion in straight lines where they are collinear.
diagram_description: Circle with object at point P; radius shown; velocity vector tangent to circle; centripetal acceleration vector pointing toward center
subject: physics

summary: Every body continues in its state of rest or uniform motion in a straight line unless compelled by an external force to act otherwise. Equivalently, if net external force is zero, acceleration is zero. This foundational law established that force is needed to change motion, not to maintain it.
diagram_description: Free-body diagram of an object at rest and another in uniform motion, showing zero net forces
subject: physics

summary: Inertia is the resistance of a body to change its state of rest or uniform motion. Galileo inferred this principle from double inclined plane experiments: on a frictionless horizontal plane, an object moves with constant velocity indefinitely.
diagram_description: Double inclined plane apparatus showing a ball rolling down one side and up the other, with decreasing slope illustration
subject: physics

summary: Acceleration occurs only when net external force is non-zero. If net force is zero, acceleration is zero and the body remains at rest or in uniform motion. This establishes the direct causal relationship between force and acceleration changes.
diagram_description: Force and acceleration vectors showing zero acceleration when forces balance
subject: physics

summary: Aristotle incorrectly claimed that external force is required to maintain motion. This view arose from everyday experience where friction opposes motion. The error was corrected by Galileo, who recognized that friction must be overcome, not that force sustains motion itself.
diagram_description: Comparison diagram: toy car with applied force versus without, showing role of friction
subject: physics

summary: The rate of change of momentum of a body is directly proportional to the applied force and takes place in the direction in which the force acts. Mathematically, F = ma where F is net external force, m is mass, and a is acceleration. This law is local—force at an instant determines acceleration at that instant only.
diagram_description: Force and acceleration vectors on a body showing F = ma relationship with direction alignment
subject: physics

summary: Momentum is the product of a body's mass and velocity: p = mv. It is a vector quantity indicating the quantity of motion. Greater momentum requires greater force to produce the same change in velocity over the same time interval.
diagram_description: Comparison of two objects with different masses and velocities showing momentum vectors
subject: physics

summary: Force is proportional to the product of mass and acceleration (F = ma). In SI units, 1 newton is the force that accelerates 1 kg at 1 m/s². For constant mass, force is directly proportional to acceleration; greater force produces greater acceleration.
diagram_description: Graph showing linear relationship between force and acceleration for constant mass
subject: physics

summary: Newton's Second Law is a vector equation equivalent to three component equations: Fx = max, Fy = may, Fz = maz. If force is not parallel to velocity, only the velocity component along the force direction changes; perpendicular components remain unchanged (e.g., horizontal velocity in projectile motion).
diagram_description: Force components showing how force affects motion along its direction while perpendicular components remain constant
subject: physics

summary: Impulse is the product of force and time duration, equal to the change in momentum: Impulse = F·Δt = Δp. When large forces act for very short times, impulse is more practical to measure than individual force and time values, as in ball-wall collisions.
diagram_description: Timeline showing force pulse during collision and resulting momentum change
subject: physics

summary: To every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. Forces always occur in pairs between two bodies: the force on body A by body B equals and opposes the force on body B by body A. These forces act simultaneously and on different bodies.
diagram_description: Two interacting bodies showing equal and opposite force vectors between them
subject: physics

summary: Action and reaction forces are simultaneous mutual forces between a pair of bodies. There is no cause-effect relationship: neither causes the other. They act on different bodies and cannot be added to cancel out. Internal action-reaction pairs within a system sum to zero.
diagram_description: Two objects in contact showing action force on one and reaction force on the other, labeled clearly
subject: physics

summary: The total momentum of an isolated system of particles is conserved. In a system with no external forces, internal action-reaction forces cause momentum changes in individual particles, but these changes cancel in pairs and total momentum remains constant. Applies to all collisions—elastic or inelastic.
diagram_description: Before and after diagrams of a collision showing momentum vectors summing to equal values
subject: physics

summary: Friction is the component of contact force parallel to surfaces, opposing impending or actual relative motion. Static friction (fs ≤ μs N) opposes impending motion; kinetic friction (fk = μk N) opposes actual motion. Both are independent of contact area and satisfy empirical laws with coefficients μs and μk.
diagram_description: Block on surface showing friction force direction and normal reaction, with applied force causing impending motion
subject: physics

summary: Static friction opposes impending (potential but prevented) relative motion between surfaces. It is self-adjusting, increasing with applied force up to a maximum value (fs)max = μs N. The coefficient μs depends only on surface nature. Without static friction reaching its limit, the body remains at rest.
diagram_description: Block on surface with increasing applied force showing static friction increasing in steps until it reaches maximum
subject: physics

summary: Kinetic friction opposes actual relative motion between surfaces in contact. Its magnitude fk = μk N is independent of velocity and contact area, depending only on surface nature. Coefficient μk is always less than μs. Once motion begins, kinetic friction replaces static friction.
diagram_description: Block sliding on surface with constant kinetic friction force shown independent of speed variations
subject: physics

summary: Rolling friction is resistance to rolling motion caused by surface deformation creating a finite contact area. It is 2–3 orders of magnitude smaller than static or sliding friction for the same weight, explaining why wheels are more efficient. Origin differs from static/kinetic friction though both involve contact forces.
diagram_description: Wheel rolling on surface showing deformed contact area and resulting rolling friction force
subject: physics

summary: Normal reaction is the component of contact force perpendicular to the surfaces in contact, directed away from the surface. It is self-adjusting and typically equals weight for a body at rest on a horizontal surface. It adjusts based on other forces and acceleration to maintain contact.
diagram_description: Block on horizontal surface showing weight downward and normal reaction upward, in equilibrium
formula: N = mg\text{ (at rest on horizontal surface)}
subject: physics

summary: Circular motion requires a centripetal force directed toward the center to provide the necessary centripetal acceleration. For uniform circular motion with speed v and radius R, centripetal acceleration is a = v²/R directed toward the center. The force providing this is fc = mv²/R.
diagram_description: Circle showing velocity tangent at a point and centripetal acceleration/force directed inward toward center
subject: physics

summary: Centripetal force is any force directed toward the center providing inward radial acceleration for circular motion. Magnitude is fc = mv²/R; direction is always toward the center. The centripetal force may be provided by tension, friction, gravity, or normal force depending on context.
diagram_description: Stone rotating on string showing centripetal force pointing toward center and velocity tangent to circle
subject: physics

summary: On a level road, friction alone provides the centripetal force for circular turning. Maximum speed is vmax = √(μs Rg) where R is turn radius. Beyond this speed, friction cannot provide sufficient centripetal force and the car slips outward, independent of mass.
diagram_description: Top view of car on flat road showing friction force pointing toward center of circular path
subject: physics

summary: On a banked road tilted at angle θ, both normal reaction and friction contribute to centripetal force. Optimum speed vo = √(Rg tan θ) requires no friction. Maximum permissible speed vmax = √[Rg(μs + tan θ)/(1 − μs tan θ)] is greater than on flat road. Banking reduces tire wear at optimal speed.
diagram_description: Side view of car on banked road showing normal reaction, friction, weight, and resultant centripetal force direction
subject: physics

summary: A particle is in equilibrium when the net external force on it is zero, meaning it is either at rest or in uniform motion. For equilibrium under concurrent forces, the vector sum of all forces must be zero. This satisfies translational equilibrium of the particle.
diagram_description: Multiple force vectors meeting at a point, arranged to form a closed polygon representing equilibrium
subject: physics

summary: For equilibrium: F1 + F2 = 0 (two forces), or F1 + F2 + F3 = 0 (three forces). Equivalent to component equations: Fx + Fy + Fz = 0 along x, y, z axes independently. A particle is in equilibrium when acceleration is zero, satisfying both the first and second laws of motion.
diagram_description: Two and three force equilibrium diagrams with component breakdowns along coordinate axes
subject: physics

summary: Three concurrent forces in equilibrium can be represented by the sides of a triangle with vector arrows in the same sense. The resultant of any two forces (by parallelogram law) equals and opposes the third force. This generalizes to n forces forming a closed n-sided polygon in equilibrium.
diagram_description: Three force vectors arranged to form a closed triangle showing equilibrium; also shows parallelogram construction
subject: physics

summary: Ancient Indian thinkers recognized different types of force (continuous pressure, impact, persistence tendency) and the concept of vega—tendency to move in a straight line—which parallels inertia. Bhaskara (1150 AD) introduced instantaneous motion concept, anticipating calculus-based velocity definitions.
subject: physics

summary: Contact forces arise from physical contact between bodies (friction, normal reaction, tension, buoyancy). Non-contact forces act at a distance without intervening medium (gravity, magnetic, electrical). All contact forces in mechanics fundamentally originate from electrical forces between atomic constituents.
diagram_description: Two columns showing examples of contact forces (block on table, pushed ball) and non-contact forces (magnet attracting iron, Earth-Moon gravity)
subject: physics

summary: A spring exerts a restoring force proportional to displacement: F = −kx, where k is the force constant and x is displacement from equilibrium. The negative sign indicates force opposes displacement. This law holds for small displacements and is fundamental to oscillatory motion.
diagram_description: Compressed and extended springs showing restoring force arrows pointing back to equilibrium position
subject: physics

summary: Tension is the restoring force in a string or rope when stretched. For a massless, inextensible string, tension is uniform throughout and acts along the string length. Tension adjusts to maintain the constraint that the string length cannot change, based on applied forces and motion.
diagram_description: String supporting a hanging mass showing tension force arrows upward on the mass and downward on the support
subject: physics

summary: A free-body diagram isolates a system showing all external forces acting on it but not internal forces. This method systematically applies Newton's laws by clearly identifying which forces act on a chosen body, separating it from the environment and other bodies in the assembly.
diagram_description: Example of object with all external forces labeled (weight, normal, friction, applied force) without showing internal structure
subject: physics

summary: Effective mechanics problem-solving follows: (1) Draw schematic of all bodies and connections; (2) Select a system; (3) Draw free-body diagram with all external forces; (4) Identify known and unknown information; (5) Apply Newton's laws to generate equations; (6) Use constraint conditions and solve.
subject: physics

summary: The scalar product (dot product) of two vectors A and B is defined as A·B = AB cos θ, where θ is the angle between them. It yields a scalar quantity equal to the product of one vector's magnitude and the component of the other along its direction. Scalar products obey commutative and distributive laws: A·B = B·A and A·(B + C) = A·B + A·C.
diagram_description: Two vectors A and B at angle θ, showing projection of one onto the other
formula: A \cdot B = A_x B_x + A_y B_y + A_z B_z
subject: physics

summary: Covers: Work, Work-energy Theorem for Constant Forces, Scalar Product of Vectors, Kinetic Energy.
subject: physics

summary: Work is the product of the component of force in the direction of displacement and the magnitude of displacement: W = F·d = Fd cos θ. Work is positive when force and displacement are in the same direction, negative when opposite, and zero when perpendicular. It has dimensions [ML²T⁻²] and SI unit joule (J).
diagram_description: Object with force F at angle θ to displacement d, showing force component Fd cos θ
subject: physics

summary: The work-energy theorem states that the change in kinetic energy equals the net work done on the body: ΔK = Kf - Ki = W. This relates work to the change in kinetic energy regardless of the path taken, providing an integral form of Newton's second law.
diagram_description: Graph or diagram showing kinetic energy change with work applied
subject: physics

summary: Kinetic energy is the energy possessed by an object due to its motion, defined as K = ½mv². It is a scalar quantity that measures the work an object can do by virtue of its motion. Kinetic energy increases with both mass and the square of velocity.
diagram_description: Table showing kinetic energies of various objects (car, athlete, bullet, etc.)
subject: physics

summary: Covers: Variable Force Calculation, Work-energy Theorem for Variable Forces.
subject: physics

summary: Work done by a variable force F(x) is calculated by integrating force over displacement: W = ∫F(x)dx from xi to xf. For small displacements, work is approximated as ΔW = F(x)Δx, and the total work equals the area under the F-x curve.
diagram_description: Force versus displacement graph with shaded area under curve representing work done
subject: physics

summary: For variable forces, the work-energy theorem is proved by integrating Newton's second law over displacement: Kf - Ki = ∫F(x)dx. This integral form shows the change in kinetic energy equals work done, whether force is constant or variable.
diagram_description: Mathematical derivation showing dK = Fdx and integration from xi to xf
subject: physics

summary: Covers: Conservative Force Definition, Potential Energy Function, Gravitational Potential Energy, Elastic Potential Energy of Springs.
subject: physics

summary: A force is conservative if work done depends only on initial and final positions, not on the path taken. Equivalently, work done in a closed path is zero. For conservative forces, a potential energy function V(x) can be defined such that F(x) = -dV/dx.
diagram_description: Diagram showing object moving between two points via different paths with equal work
subject: physics

summary: Potential energy V(x) is the 'stored energy' due to position or configuration of a body under a conservative force. It is defined such that the force equals the negative derivative of potential energy: F(x) = -dV/dx. The change in potential energy equals negative work done by the force.
diagram_description: Potential energy curve showing relationship between force and position
subject: physics

summary: For an object of mass m at height h above Earth's surface (where h << RE), gravitational potential energy is V(h) = mgh, where g is taken as constant. The zero of potential energy is chosen at Earth's surface for convenience.
diagram_description: Ball at various heights showing increase in gravitational potential energy
subject: physics

summary: For an ideal spring, the elastic force obeys Hooke's law: Fs = -kx. The potential energy is V(x) = ½kx², where k is the spring constant and x is displacement from equilibrium. The spring force is conservative, and energy converts between kinetic and elastic potential energy.
diagram_description: Spring at equilibrium, stretched, and compressed, with force and potential energy curves
subject: physics

summary: Covers: Conservation of Total Mechanical Energy, Mechanical Energy with Non-conservative Forces.
subject: physics

summary: The total mechanical energy E = K + V remains constant when only conservative forces act on a system. Kinetic and potential energies can convert into each other, but their sum is constant: Ki + V(xi) = Kf + V(xf). This is a consequence of the work-energy theorem for conservative forces.
diagram_description: Energy conversion diagram showing kinetic and potential energy curves that sum to constant total energy
subject: physics

summary: When non-conservative forces (e.g., friction) act on a system, mechanical energy is not conserved. The change in mechanical energy equals the work done by non-conservative forces: Ef - Ei = Wnc. This modified energy conservation accounts for energy dissipated as heat or sound.
diagram_description: Diagram showing object on rough surface with friction force and mechanical energy decreasing
subject: physics

summary: Power is the time rate of doing work or transferring energy. Average power is Pav = W/t, and instantaneous power is P = dW/dt = F·v. Power has dimensions [ML²T⁻³] with SI unit watt (W), where 1 W = 1 J/s. It can also be expressed in horse-power (hp), where 1 hp = 746 W.
diagram_description: Graph showing work increasing with time at different rates to illustrate different power values
subject: physics

summary: Covers: Momentum Conservation in Collisions, Elastic Collisions, Inelastic Collisions, Collisions in One Dimension.
subject: physics

summary: In all collisions, total linear momentum is conserved because internal impulsive forces follow Newton's third law (equal and opposite). During collision time Δt, the momentum change Δp1 = -Δp2, so total initial momentum equals total final momentum: p1i + p2i = p1f + p2f.
diagram_description: Two objects before and after collision showing momentum vectors
subject: physics

summary: In an elastic collision, both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved. The deformation during collision is fully relieved with no energy loss: Kf = Ki and pf = pi. For a head-on elastic collision of m1 with stationary m2: v1f = (m1-m2)/(m1+m2)·v1i and v2f = 2m1/(m1+m2)·v1i.
diagram_description: Two objects before and after elastic collision, showing velocity vectors and equal kinetic energy
subject: physics

summary: In an inelastic collision, momentum is conserved but kinetic energy is not—some energy converts to heat, sound, or deformation. A completely inelastic collision occurs when bodies stick together after impact: m1v1i = (m1+m2)vf. The kinetic energy loss is ΔK = (m1m2)/(2(m1+m2))·v1i².
diagram_description: Two objects before and after completely inelastic collision, showing bodies moving together with reduced kinetic energy
subject: physics

summary: One-dimensional (head-on) collisions occur when all velocities lie along the same line. For elastic collisions, final velocities are derived from momentum and energy conservation. For completely inelastic collisions, both objects move with the same final velocity along the collision axis.
diagram_description: Two objects moving along a line before and after head-on collision
subject: physics

summary: In two-dimensional collisions, momentum is conserved in both x and y directions. With four unknowns (v1f, v2f, θ1, θ2) but only two momentum equations, at least one scattering angle must be known experimentally. For elastic collisions, a third energy equation helps, but one unknown still requires measurement.
diagram_description: Two objects colliding at angles, showing velocity vectors before and after collision in x-y plane
formula: m_1 v_{1i} = m_1 v_{1f} \cos \theta_1 + m_2 v_{2f} \cos \theta_2
subject: physics

subtopic_names: Pure Rotational Motion About Fixed Axis
summary: A rigid body has fixed distances between all particles. Motion can be pure translation (all particles move with same velocity), pure rotation about a fixed axis (particles move in circles), or a combination. Understanding rigid body motion bridges single-particle kinematics to extended systems.
diagram_description: Diagram showing a block sliding, a cylinder rotating, and a cylinder rolling simultaneously
subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

summary: All particles of a rigid body move with the same velocity at any instant, so the body does not rotate. The orientation of the body remains constant. Example: a block sliding down an inclined plane without rotating.
diagram_description: A block moving down an inclined plane with velocity vectors shown at different points along the block
subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

concept_name: Pure Rotational Motion About Fixed Axis
summary: Every particle moves in a circle perpendicular to a fixed axis, with center on the axis. All particles have the same angular velocity. Particles on the axis remain stationary. Examples: ceiling fan, potter's wheel, rotating merry-go-round.
diagram_description: Cross-section of rotating body showing concentric circles with center on fixed axis and angular displacement θ

builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

summary: Body undergoes both translational motion of center of mass and rotational motion about center of mass simultaneously. Example: a cylinder rolling down an incline where different points have different velocities. Rolling without slipping is a special case where translational and rotational motions are kinematically linked.
diagram_description: Cylinder rolling down incline showing center of mass translation and particle rotation with velocity vectors

builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

builds_upon.concept_name: Pure Rotational Motion About Fixed Axis
builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subtopic_names: Centre of Mass Definition and Calculation
Centre of Mass for Continuous Bodies
Centre of Gravity and Its Relation to Centre of Mass
Centre of Mass Motion Under External Forces
summary: The centre of mass is the weighted average position of all particles in a system. It is calculated as R = (Σmᵢrᵢ)/M. For a system of particles, the centre of mass moves as if all external forces act at this point, independent of internal forces.
diagram_description: Two-particle system showing masses m₁ and m₂ with position vectors and center of mass location
subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

concept_name: Centre of Mass Definition and Calculation
summary: For discrete particles, the position vector of the centre of mass is R = (Σmᵢrᵢ)/M. For two particles of equal mass, the CM lies midway between them. This concept extends to three dimensions and forms the foundation for analyzing system motion.
diagram_description: Number line showing two masses with position vectors and center of mass marked at weighted position
subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

concept_name: Centre of Mass for Continuous Bodies
summary: For continuous bodies with uniform mass distribution, CM is found using integration: R = (1/M)∫r dm. For symmetric homogeneous bodies (spheres, cylinders, rings, rods), the CM coincides with the geometric centre due to reflection symmetry.
diagram_description: Thin rod with infinitesimal mass elements dm shown symmetrically positioned about center

builds_upon.concept_name: Centre of Mass Definition and Calculation
builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

concept_name: Centre of Gravity and Its Relation to Centre of Mass
summary: The centre of gravity is the point where total gravitational torque on a body is zero: Σ(rᵢ × mᵢg) = 0. In uniform gravity (g constant throughout body), centre of gravity coincides with centre of mass. For extended bodies in non-uniform fields, they differ.
diagram_description: Irregular cardboard balanced on pencil point with center of gravity marked and multiple suspension points shown

builds_upon.concept_name: Centre of Mass Definition and Calculation
builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

concept_name: Centre of Mass Motion Under External Forces
summary: The CM moves according to MA = F_ext, where M is total mass and F_ext is sum of external forces. Internal forces do not affect CM motion. This applies to any system, including rigid bodies with internal motion or particles with all kinds of dynamics.
diagram_description: Exploding projectile showing parabolic path of center of mass continuing unchanged while fragments scatter

builds_upon.concept_name: Centre of Mass Definition and Calculation
builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

summary: Total linear momentum of a system P = Σmᵢvᵢ = MV equals total mass times centre of mass velocity. When total external force is zero, total momentum is conserved: dP/dt = 0. This law applies to collisions, explosions, and radioactive decay.
diagram_description: Diagram showing radium nucleus decaying into radon and alpha particle with momentum vectors conserved
subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

summary: The total linear momentum of a system is the vector sum of individual momenta: P = Σmᵢvᵢ. This equals the product of total mass and centre of mass velocity: P = MV. Momentum is a vector quantity with dimensions MLT⁻¹.
diagram_description: Multiple objects with different masses and velocities showing individual momentum vectors and total system momentum
subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

summary: When the net external force on a system is zero, total linear momentum remains constant: P = constant. This applies to radioactive decay, explosions, collisions, and binary star systems where internal forces redistribute momentum but total momentum is unchanged.
diagram_description: Before and after diagram of two-body collision or decay showing momentum vectors summing to same total
formula: P_x = c_1, \quad P_y = c_2, \quad P_z = c_3

builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subtopic_names: Definition and Magnitude of Cross Product
Properties and Applications of Cross Product
summary: The cross product of vectors a and b produces vector c with magnitude c = ab sinθ, perpendicular to the plane of a and b. Direction is given by right-hand rule. Cross products are non-commutative (a × b = −b × a) and distributive over addition.
diagram_description: Two vectors a and b at angle θ with resulting perpendicular cross product and right-hand rule demonstration
subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

concept_name: Definition and Magnitude of Cross Product
summary: For vectors a and b, the magnitude of cross product is c = ab sinθ where θ is angle between them. Equivalently, c = ab⊥ where b⊥ is component of b perpendicular to a. The cross product is zero if vectors are parallel (θ = 0° or 180°).
diagram_description: Two vectors showing angle θ and perpendicular height component b⊥ with magnitude calculation labeled
subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

summary: The cross product c is perpendicular to the plane containing a and b. Direction is determined by the right-hand rule: curl fingers from a to b, thumb points in direction of a × b. Alternatively, a right-handed screw advances in the direction of c.
diagram_description: Right-hand shown curling from vector a to vector b with thumb pointing in cross product direction

builds_upon.concept_name: Definition and Magnitude of Cross Product
builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

concept_name: Properties and Applications of Cross Product
summary: Cross product is non-commutative: a × b = −b × a. It is distributive: a × (b + c) = a × b + a × c. In component form, a × b can be evaluated using determinant. Used to define torque, angular momentum, and linear velocity in rotation.
diagram_description: Three-dimensional coordinate system showing unit vector cross products with determinant notation
formula: \mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ a_x & a_y & a_z \\ b_x & b_y & b_z \end{vmatrix}

builds_upon.concept_name: Definition and Magnitude of Cross Product
builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subtopic_names: Kinematic Equations for Constant Angular Acceleration
summary: Rotational kinematics describes motion of rotating bodies using angular quantities analogous to linear motion. Angular displacement θ is measured from fixed direction, angular velocity ω = dθ/dt, and angular acceleration α = dω/dt. For constant angular acceleration, kinematic equations parallel linear motion equations.
diagram_description: Rigid body rotating about fixed axis showing angular displacement θ at different times
subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

summary: Angular displacement θ is measured in radians from fixed reference direction in plane of motion. Angular velocity is vector ω = dθ/dt directed along rotation axis (right-hand rule). All particles of rotating body have same angular velocity at any instant, distinguishing rotation from translation.
diagram_description: Rotating body showing angular displacement θ, angular velocity vector ω along axis, and particle positions
subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

aliases: Rate of Change of Angular Velocity
summary: Angular acceleration α is the time rate of change of angular velocity: α = dω/dt. For fixed-axis rotation, α is a scalar (along axis direction). It characterizes how fast angular velocity is changing, analogous to linear acceleration in translational motion.
diagram_description: Graph showing angular velocity increasing linearly with time, with slope representing angular acceleration

builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

concept_name: Kinematic Equations for Constant Angular Acceleration
summary: For constant angular acceleration, three kinematic equations apply: ω = ω₀ + αt, θ = θ₀ + ω₀t + ½αt², and ω² = ω₀² + 2α(θ − θ₀). These exactly parallel linear kinematic equations with angular quantities replacing linear quantities.
diagram_description: Table comparing linear and rotational kinematic equations side by side

builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subtopic_names: Angular Momentum of Particle and System
Relation Between Torque and Angular Momentum
summary: Torque τ = r × F is rotational analogue of force; it produces angular acceleration. Angular momentum L = r × p is rotational analogue of linear momentum. The fundamental relation dL/dt = τ_ext shows that external torque changes system's angular momentum. When τ_ext = 0, angular momentum is conserved.
diagram_description: Force applied at distance from pivot showing position vector, force vector, and resulting torque
subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

summary: Torque τ = r × F is a vector with magnitude τ = rF sinθ = r⊥F where r⊥ is perpendicular distance from axis to line of action of force. Torque is rotational analogue of force; it causes angular acceleration. SI unit is newton-metre (N m). Torque vanishes if force passes through origin or is parallel to position vector.
diagram_description: Door with force applied at different distances and angles showing how torque varies with lever arm length

builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

concept_name: Angular Momentum of Particle and System
summary: Angular momentum of particle is L = r × p with magnitude L = rp sinθ = r⊥p. For system of n particles, total angular momentum is L = Σ(rᵢ × pᵢ). Angular momentum is rotational analogue of linear momentum. Vanishes if particle is at origin or momentum is directed toward origin.
diagram_description: Particle moving in circular path showing position vector, linear momentum, and angular momentum vectors

builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

concept_name: Relation Between Torque and Angular Momentum
summary: The time rate of change of angular momentum equals external torque: dL/dt = τ_ext. This is rotational analogue of Newton's second law (dP/dt = F_ext). For particle with constant velocity, angular momentum about any point is constant (zero external torque).
diagram_description: Particle moving with constant velocity showing that angular momentum about arbitrary point remains constant

builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

builds_upon.concept_name: Angular Momentum of Particle and System
builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

aliases: Law of Conservation of Angular Momentum
summary: When total external torque is zero, total angular momentum of system is conserved: L = constant, or Iω = constant. This applies to rotating ice skaters (stretching/folding arms), acrobats, spinning tops, and binary star systems. Conservation is independent of whether system is rigid body or has internal motion.
diagram_description: Ice skater with arms outstretched spinning slowly, then arms folded spinning faster, showing angular momentum conservation

builds_upon.concept_name: Angular Momentum of Particle and System
builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

summary: A rigid body is in mechanical equilibrium if (1) total external force is zero (ΣF = 0) for translational equilibrium, and (2) total external torque is zero (Στ = 0) for rotational equilibrium. Both conditions must hold for complete equilibrium. This applies to bridges, levers, seesaws, and balanced structures.
diagram_description: Rigid rod supported at fulcrum with forces and torques labeled showing equilibrium conditions
subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

summary: For translational equilibrium: ΣFₓ = 0, ΣFᵧ = 0, ΣFᵤ = 0 (three scalar equations from vector condition ΣF = 0). For rotational equilibrium: Στₓ = 0, Στᵧ = 0, Στᵤ = 0 (three scalar equations from vector condition Στ = 0). For coplanar forces, only two force equations and one torque equation are needed.
diagram_description: Free body diagram of beam with multiple forces and support reactions showing component directions

builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

summary: Complete mechanical equilibrium requires both ΣF = 0 and Στ = 0. Partial equilibrium occurs when only one condition holds: translational but not rotational, or rotational but not translational. For equilibrium of rigid body, torque is independent of origin choice if and only if ΣF = 0.
diagram_description: Rod with two parallel forces in opposite directions showing rotational equilibrium but not translational

builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

summary: For a balanced lever: d₁F₁ = d₂F₂, where d₁ is load arm, F₁ is load, d₂ is effort arm, F₂ is effort. Mechanical advantage M.A. = F₁/F₂ = d₂/d₁. If effort arm exceeds load arm (d₂ > d₁), a small effort can lift a large load. Applied to seesaws, balances, crowbars, and other simple machines.
diagram_description: Lever system with fulcrum, load, effort, and distance labels showing torque balance

builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

summary: A couple consists of two equal and opposite parallel forces with different lines of action. Couple produces pure torque (rotation without translation): τ = (AB) × F where AB is vector between force application points. Moment of couple is independent of origin choice. Examples: opening bottle lid, compass needle in magnetic field.
diagram_description: Two equal opposite forces at different points on a body showing couple production and torque direction

builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subtopic_names: Definition and Physical Meaning of Moment of Inertia
Moment of Inertia for Standard Shapes
summary: Moment of inertia I = Σmᵢrᵢ² measures body's resistance to angular acceleration about an axis. It is rotational analogue of mass in linear motion. Unlike mass, I depends on mass distribution and choice of axis. Larger I means greater resistance to rotational acceleration. Used in rotational kinetic energy: K = ½Iω².
diagram_description: Rotating body showing different particles at different distances from axis with mass and distance labels
subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

concept_name: Definition and Physical Meaning of Moment of Inertia
summary: Moment of inertia I = Σmᵢrᵢ² where rᵢ is perpendicular distance of mass mᵢ from rotation axis. Dimensions: ML². SI units: kg m². As measure of rotational inertia, I resists changes in angular motion just as mass resists changes in linear motion. Depends on mass, body shape, and axis location/orientation.
diagram_description: Multiple particles at different distances from central axis with radii and masses labeled
subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

concept_name: Moment of Inertia for Standard Shapes
summary: Standard shapes have known moments of inertia: thin ring (I = MR²), solid cylinder (I = ½MR²), solid sphere (I = ⅖MR²), thin rod about center (I = ML²/12), disk about diameter (I = ¼MR²). These formulas apply for homogeneous bodies and specified axes. Useful for calculating I of composite bodies.
diagram_description: Table showing various 3D shapes with their moment of inertia formulas for different axes

builds_upon.concept_name: Definition and Physical Meaning of Moment of Inertia
builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

summary: Radius of gyration k is defined by I = Mk², where M is total mass. It represents distance from axis at which entire mass could be concentrated to produce same moment of inertia. For rod about center: k = L/√12. For disk about diameter: k = R/2. It characterizes how mass is distributed about rotation axis.
diagram_description: Composite body showing actual mass distribution and equivalent point mass at radius k giving same I

builds_upon.concept_name: Definition and Physical Meaning of Moment of Inertia
builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subtopic_names: Work Done by Torque and Rotational Power
summary: Newton's second law for rotation: τ = Iα relates torque, moment of inertia, and angular acceleration. Work done by torque: W = ∫τ dθ. Rotational power: P = τω. These equations parallel F = ma, W = ∫F ds, P = Fv from linear motion, with torque replacing force and moment of inertia replacing mass.
diagram_description: Rotating disk with applied torque causing angular acceleration, with work and power elements shown
subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

summary: For rigid body rotating about fixed axis: τ = Iα where τ is net external torque, I is moment of inertia, and α is angular acceleration. This is rotational analogue of F = ma. Angular acceleration is directly proportional to applied torque and inversely proportional to moment of inertia.
diagram_description: Rotating body with applied torque force showing resulting angular acceleration and relationship to moment of inertia

builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
System of Particles and Rotational Motion
System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

concept_name: Work Done by Torque and Rotational Power
summary: Work done by net torque on rotating body: W = ∫τ dθ. For constant torque: W = τ × Δθ. Instantaneous power: P = τω. Work-energy theorem states work done equals change in kinetic energy: W = ½Iω² − ½Iω₀². These equations parallel linear motion with torque replacing force and angular displacement replacing linear displacement.
diagram_description: Rotating body with applied torque showing angular displacement and power transmission

builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

summary: Kinetic energy of rotating body: K = ½Iω². This is rotational analogue of translational K = ½mv². For body with both translation and rotation: K_total = ½MV² + ½Iω² where V is velocity of centre of mass. Moment of inertia plays same role in rotation as mass does in translation.
diagram_description: Rotating body showing kinetic energy as function of moment of inertia and angular velocity

builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

concept_name: Linear Velocity and Angular Velocity Relation
parent_concept_name: Angular Momentum in Fixed Axis Rotation
summary: For particle in rigid body rotating about fixed axis: v = ω × r where r is position vector from origin on axis. Magnitude: v = ωr⊥ where r⊥ is perpendicular distance from axis. Linear velocity is always tangent to circular path. This relation holds for both fixed-axis rotation and rotation with one point fixed.
diagram_description: Rotating body showing angular velocity vector along axis and linear velocity vectors tangent to particle paths
subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

concept_name: Vector Relation Between V and Ω
parent_concept_name: Angular Momentum in Fixed Axis Rotation
summary: Linear velocity vector v = ω × r is perpendicular to both ω (along axis) and r (position vector). Direction is tangent to circular path by right-hand rule. Magnitude is v = ωr sinθ = ωr⊥. Relation applies to rotation about fixed axis or more general rotation with one point fixed. Allows calculating velocity of any point from angular velocity.
diagram_description: 3D diagram showing angular velocity vector, position vector, and resulting linear velocity from cross product

builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

concept_name: Angular Momentum in Fixed Axis Rotation
subtopic_names: Angular Momentum Component Along Rotation Axis
Angular Momentum Conservation in Fixed Axis Rotation
Linear Velocity and Angular Velocity Relation
Vector Relation Between V and Ω
summary: For rigid body rotating about fixed axis: the component of angular momentum along axis is L_z = Iω. For symmetric bodies (axis is symmetry axis): total L = Iω is parallel to ω. For asymmetric bodies, only L_z component is along axis; perpendicular component L⊥ exists. Total angular momentum relates to kinetic energy through L = Iω for symmetric cases.
diagram_description: Symmetric rotating body showing angular momentum vector along rotation axis parallel to ω
subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

concept_name: Angular Momentum Component Along Rotation Axis
parent_concept_name: Angular Momentum in Fixed Axis Rotation
summary: General angular momentum: L = L_z + L⊥ where L_z is component along rotation axis and L⊥ is perpendicular. For symmetric bodies (rotation axis is axis of symmetry): L⊥ = 0 and L = L_z = Iω. For asymmetric bodies: only L_z = Iω is along axis. Relation dL_z/dt = τ governs rotational dynamics about fixed axis.
diagram_description: Asymmetric rotating body showing angular momentum components parallel and perpendicular to rotation axis

builds_upon.concept_name: Angular Momentum of Particle and System
builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

concept_name: Angular Momentum Conservation in Fixed Axis Rotation
parent_concept_name: Angular Momentum in Fixed Axis Rotation
aliases: Conservation of L in Fixed Axis Rotation
summary: For fixed axis rotation with zero external torque: L_z = Iω = constant. If moment of inertia changes (e.g., stretching arms while spinning), angular velocity adjusts to conserve angular momentum: I₁ω₁ = I₂ω₂. Example: ice skater spinning faster when pulling arms in. Explains pirouette, acrobatic maneuvers, and precession phenomena.
diagram_description: Ice skater or dancer showing before and after configuration with moment of inertia and angular velocity changes

builds_upon.concept_name: Angular Momentum Component Along Rotation Axis
builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

summary: In real rotating bodies, friction at pivots and bearings creates resistive torque that opposes rotation. For rolling bodies, rolling resistance depends on contact properties. Rolling without slipping condition: v = ωr relates translational and rotational motion kinematically. This constraint connects dynamics of translation and rotation for rolling bodies.
diagram_description: Rolling cylinder on surface showing friction force and torque with velocity and angular velocity vectors

builds_upon.concept_name: Pure Rotational Motion About Fixed Axis
builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

summary: When gravitational torque acts on spinning body (e.g., top), angular momentum vector precesses about vertical. Precession occurs because dL/dt = τ causes L to change direction at constant rate. Precession angular velocity: Ω = τ/L = Mgd/(Iω). Examples include spinning tops, gyroscopes, and Earth's axial precession. Demonstrates that external torque changes angular momentum direction, not just magnitude.
diagram_description: Spinning top showing gravitational torque and precession of angular momentum vector around vertical

builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

summary: A flywheel is a high-inertia disk used in machinery to smooth rotational motion by resisting sudden speed changes. Large moment of inertia stores significant rotational kinetic energy and releases it gradually. Used in engines, pumps, and vehicles to prevent jerky motion and provide smooth power delivery. Energy stored: E = ½Iω².
diagram_description: Cross-section of engine with large flywheel disk attached to crankshaft showing energy storage and release

builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

concept_name: Binary Star Systems and Orbital Mechanics
summary: In binary star systems with no external forces, centre of mass moves uniformly in straight line (conserved momentum). In centre of mass frame, two stars orbit the CM in circles with diametrically opposite positions. Trajectories in lab frame are combination of CM translation and circular orbits. Demonstrates practical application of centre of mass and momentum conservation concepts.
diagram_description: Double-star system showing CM moving in straight line while stars orbit around it in opposite circles

builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

concept_name: Radioactive Decay in Centre of Mass Frame
summary: In radioactive decay like Ra → Rn + α, internal forces release energy but external forces are negligible, so momentum is conserved. In CM frame, decay products move in opposite directions with equal momentum magnitudes. In lab frame, CM motion combines with this relative motion. Demonstrates how CM frame simplifies analysis of particle reactions.
diagram_description: Radium nucleus decaying into radon and alpha showing particle velocities in lab and CM frames

builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics
chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

summary: Newton's law states that every body attracts every other body with a force proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them. The gravitational force is always attractive and acts along the line joining the two masses. For extended objects, forces from individual mass elements must be summed vectorially.
diagram_description: Vector diagram showing gravitational forces between two point masses with distance and direction labeled
subject: physics

summary: Every body in the universe attracts every other body with a force directly proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them. This foundational law explains both terrestrial gravity and planetary motion. The gravitational constant G = 6.67×10⁻¹¹ N m²/kg².
subject: physics

summary: The gravitational force on a point mass m₂ due to m₁ has magnitude F = Gm₁m₂/r² and is directed along the line joining them. In vector form, the force is attractive, pointing from m₂ toward m₁. By Newton's third law, F₁₂ = −F₂₁.
diagram_description: Two point masses with force vectors showing attraction along the line joining them
subject: physics

summary: The resultant gravitational force on one mass due to multiple masses is the vector sum of individual forces from each mass. Each force acts independently and uninfluenced by other bodies. This allows calculation of net gravitational effects in complex systems.
diagram_description: Multiple masses with individual force vectors on a central mass combining into a resultant force
subject: physics

summary: For a hollow spherical shell of uniform density: a point mass outside experiences force as if all shell mass is at the center; a point inside experiences zero force. For a solid uniform sphere, the force on an external point acts as if all mass is concentrated at the center, while internal points only feel attraction from the interior sphere.
diagram_description: Cross-section of hollow and solid spheres showing force vectors inside and outside regions
subject: physics

summary: Kepler's three laws describe planetary motion: all planets orbit in ellipses with the Sun at one focus, the radius vector sweeps equal areas in equal times (conserving angular momentum), and the square of the period is proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis. These laws were derived from observational data and later explained by Newton's law of gravitation.
diagram_description: Elliptical orbit showing two foci, semi-major axis, and area-sweep illustration
subject: physics

summary: All planets move in elliptical orbits with the Sun at one focus. An ellipse is a closed curve such that the sum of distances from any point to the two foci is constant. Perihelion is the closest point to the Sun; aphelion is the farthest. A circle is a special case where both foci coincide.
diagram_description: Ellipse with two foci marked, showing perihelion and aphelion positions on a planetary orbit
subject: physics

summary: The radius vector from the Sun to a planet sweeps out equal areas in equal time intervals. This reflects conservation of angular momentum: since the gravitational force is central (directed toward the Sun), angular momentum about the Sun remains constant. Planets move faster near perihelion and slower near aphelion.
diagram_description: Elliptical orbit showing equal areas swept by radius vector in equal time periods

builds_upon.concept_name: Angular Momentum of Particle and System
builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics

summary: The square of a planet's orbital period is proportional to the cube of its semi-major axis. Mathematically, T² = ka³, where k = 4π²/GM_s for the Sun's mass M_s. This law applies to any orbiting body and provides a way to determine the mass of the central body.
diagram_description: Graph of T² vs a³ showing linear relationship for planetary data
subject: physics

subtopic_names: Acceleration Due to Gravity at Earth's Surface
Variation of Gravity with Height and Depth
summary: Gravitational potential energy is the energy stored due to relative positions in a gravitational field. For two masses separated by distance r, PE = −Gm₁m₂/r (with zero at infinity). Gravitational force is conservative; work done is path-independent and equals the change in potential energy.
diagram_description: Potential energy curve showing negative well shape with distance from center
subject: physics

summary: Gravitational potential energy of a mass m at distance r from center of mass M is V = −Gm₁m₂/r, with zero assigned at infinity. Only energy differences are physically meaningful. The work done in moving a mass equals the change in potential energy: W = V₂ − V₁.
diagram_description: Graph of gravitational potential energy vs distance showing negative hyperbolic curve
subject: physics

summary: Gravitational potential is the potential energy per unit mass at a point in a gravitational field. For a mass M at distance r, U = −GM/r. It is a scalar quantity with units J/kg. The potential energy of a mass m is then V = mU.
diagram_description: Potential field around a mass with contours showing equipotential surfaces
subject: physics

concept_name: Acceleration Due to Gravity at Earth's Surface
summary: The acceleration due to gravity at Earth's surface is g = GM_E/R_E², where M_E is Earth's mass and R_E is its radius. This equals approximately 9.8 m/s². It can also be calculated from observing free-fall motion, and knowing G and R_E enables determination of Earth's mass.
diagram_description: Diagram showing gravitational force and acceleration on a mass at Earth's surface
subject: physics

concept_name: Variation of Gravity with Height and Depth
summary: At height h above Earth's surface, g(h) = GM_E/(R_E + h)² ≈ g₀(1 − 2h/R_E) for h << R_E. At depth d below surface, g(d) = GM_E(R_E − d)/R_E³ ≈ g₀(1 − d/R_E). Gravity decreases both going up and going down, reaching maximum at Earth's surface.
diagram_description: Graph showing gravity decreasing with height and depth, maximum at surface

builds_upon.concept_name: Acceleration Due to Gravity at Earth's Surface

subject: physics

summary: Orbital mechanics describes the motion of bodies under gravitational attraction. Key concepts include escape speed (minimum speed to escape gravity), satellite orbital velocity (balance of centripetal and gravitational forces), and orbital energy (sum of kinetic and potential energy, always negative for bound orbits).
diagram_description: Circular orbit showing velocity, radius, and centripetal acceleration vectors
subject: physics

summary: Escape speed is the minimum speed at which an object must be launched to escape a celestial body's gravitational influence and reach infinity. From Earth's surface, v_e = √(2GM_E/R_E) = √(2gR_E) ≈ 11.2 km/s. It is independent of the object's mass and direction of projection (if vertical).
diagram_description: Energy diagram showing kinetic and potential energy curves with escape speed marked
subject: physics

summary: For a satellite in circular orbit at distance (R_E + h) from Earth's center, gravitational force provides centripetal force: GM_E m/r² = mv²/r. This gives orbital speed v = √(GM_E/r) and period T = 2π√(r³/GM_E). For satellites very close to Earth, T₀ ≈ 85 minutes.
diagram_description: Circular orbit showing satellite velocity tangent to circle and centripetal acceleration toward center
subject: physics

summary: For a satellite in circular orbit at radius r, kinetic energy K = GM_E m/(2r), potential energy P = −GM_E m/r, and total energy E = −GM_E m/(2r). The total energy is negative (bound orbit), and |K| = |P|/2. To transfer to a higher orbit requires energy input.
diagram_description: Energy level diagram showing kinetic, potential, and total energy vs orbital radius
subject: physics

summary: The gravitational constant G is a fundamental constant appearing in Newton's law of gravitation. Its value was first measured by Henry Cavendish in 1798 using a torsion balance. The currently accepted value is G = 6.67×10⁻¹¹ N m²/kg². Knowledge of G combined with g and R_E allows determination of Earth's mass.
diagram_description: Cavendish torsion balance apparatus with suspended bar and large spheres
subject: physics

summary: Cavendish suspended a bar with small lead spheres from a fine wire and brought large lead spheres close to them. The gravitational force twisted the wire; the angle of twist was measured when restoring torque balanced gravitational torque. From τθ = FLd = GM m L/d², the value of G was calculated.
diagram_description: Schematic of Cavendish apparatus showing bar, spheres, wire suspension, and measurement scale
subject: physics

summary: The gravitational constant G has the value 6.67×10⁻¹¹ N m²/kg². Its SI units are derived from the law F = Gm₁m₂/r². This constant is the same everywhere in the universe and does not depend on the nature of the masses or the medium between them.
subject: physics

summary: The concept of gravitation evolved from ancient geocentric models through heliocentrism (Copernicus, Aryabhata) to Newton's universal law. Galileo demonstrated that all bodies fall with the same acceleration regardless of mass. Newton synthesized Kepler's laws and terrestrial gravity into a universal law explaining both planetary motion and everyday phenomena.
subject: physics

summary: The geocentric model (Ptolemy, ~2000 years ago) placed Earth at the center with all celestial objects revolving around it in circular orbits. The heliocentric model (Copernicus, 1473-1543; Aryabhata, 5th century) placed the Sun at the center. Copernicus's model was initially discredited by the church but supported by Galileo.
diagram_description: Diagram comparing geocentric model with Earth at center vs heliocentric model with Sun at center
subject: physics

summary: Galileo (1564-1642) experimentally demonstrated that all bodies fall toward Earth with the same constant acceleration regardless of mass. He conducted experiments with rolling bodies on inclined planes to study acceleration. Galileo supported Copernicus's heliocentric model despite church opposition.
subject: physics

summary: Newton unified terrestrial gravity and planetary motion by proposing that the Moon's centripetal acceleration decreases as the inverse square of distance (like the Moon-Earth distance versus Earth's radius). This insight led to his universal law of gravitation, synthesizing Kepler's laws and terrestrial phenomena into one comprehensive theory.
subject: physics

summary: Gravitational theory enables determination of Earth's mass from known g, G, and R_E. Tidal effects arise from differential gravitational forces of the Moon and Sun on Earth's oceans. Astronauts in orbiting spacecraft experience weightlessness not because gravity is absent, but because they are in free fall with their spacecraft.
subject: physics

summary: Earth's mass M_E can be determined from M_E = gR_E²/G using measured values of surface gravity g, Earth's radius R_E, and gravitational constant G. This calculation is a consequence of Newton's law: since g = GM_E/R_E², rearranging gives the mass. This is why Cavendish's measurement of G is described as 'weighing the Earth'.

builds_upon.concept_name: Acceleration Due to Gravity at Earth's Surface

subject: physics

summary: Tides result from differential gravitational forces: the near side of Earth experiences stronger gravitational pull from the Moon than the far side. Although the Sun's total gravitational force on Earth exceeds the Moon's, the Moon's tidal effect is greater because of the steeper gravitational gradient across Earth's diameter at the Moon's distance.
diagram_description: Diagram showing gravitational force gradient from Moon on near and far sides of Earth
subject: physics

summary: Astronauts in an orbiting spacecraft experience weightlessness not because Earth's gravity is absent, but because both astronaut and spacecraft are in free fall toward Earth. Gravitational force provides exactly the centripetal force needed for circular orbit. The astronaut has zero normal force (apparent weight = 0) because there is no supporting surface.
diagram_description: Diagram showing spacecraft and astronaut in orbit with gravity providing centripetal force
subject: physics
