summary: In real systems, damping (friction, air resistance) causes oscillations to decay. An external periodic force can sustain oscillations (forced oscillations). Resonance occurs when the driving frequency matches the natural frequency, causing maximum amplitude response. These phenomena are beyond ideal SHM.
subject: physics

summary: Oscillating bodies eventually come to rest due to friction and air resistance. Damped oscillation has amplitude decreasing exponentially: A(t) = A₀e^(−bt/2m). Damped SHM is strictly harmonic only for times much less than 2m/b. The system loses mechanical energy to heat.
diagram_description: Graph of damped oscillation showing exponential decay envelope around sinusoidal oscillation
subject: physics

summary: Oscillating bodies can be kept in motion by an external periodic force. Forced oscillations occur at the driving frequency. Resonance is the phenomenon where driving frequency equals natural frequency, producing maximum amplitude response. This is important in musical instruments, engines, and structural vibrations.
subject: physics

summary: The superposition of two or more SHMs produces a resultant motion that is periodic if frequency ratios are rational numbers. When frequencies are equal, the result is SHM with different amplitude and phase. When frequencies differ, the resultant is periodic but not simple harmonic (e.g., Lissajous figures).
diagram_description: Lissajous figures showing resultant patterns from superposing two perpendicular SHMs with different frequency ratios
subject: physics

summary: When two SHMs with the same frequency ω and arbitrary amplitudes/phases are superposed, the result is another SHM with the same frequency. The resultant amplitude and phase depend on the phase difference between the two motions: they combine vectorially.
subject: physics

summary: When superposing two SHMs with equal frequency ω: if phase difference is 0°, amplitudes add constructively (maximum); if 180°, they cancel (minimum). Intermediate phase differences produce intermediate amplitudes. The result is always SHM with frequency ω.
subject: physics

summary: Superposition of two SHMs with different frequencies ω₁ and ω₂ is periodic only if ω₁/ω₂ is rational. The result is not SHM but periodic motion. If frequencies are close, beats occur. Lissajous figures in 2D show rich patterns depending on frequency ratios and phase differences.
subject: physics

summary: Any material medium consists of coupled oscillators (atoms/molecules) that can oscillate collectively. These collective oscillations manifest as waves. Examples include water waves, seismic waves, and electromagnetic waves. This connection shows oscillations are fundamental to understanding wave phenomena.
subject: physics

summary: Coupled oscillators are oscillating systems that interact with each other (e.g., masses connected by springs). They exhibit normal modes where all parts oscillate at the same frequency. Energy can transfer between oscillators through the coupling.
subject: physics

summary: Oscillations of medium constituents propagate as disturbances through the medium. Sound propagates via air molecule vibrations; water waves via water particle oscillations. The collective behavior of oscillating particles produces macroscopic wave phenomena.
subject: physics

summary: Collective oscillations of medium constituents manifest as waves. Water waves result from oscillating water surface; seismic waves from oscillating Earth material; electromagnetic waves from oscillating electric and magnetic fields. This connects Chapter 13 (Oscillations) to Chapter 14 (Waves).
subject: physics

summary: Waves are patterns of disturbance that propagate through a medium without bulk motion of matter. Transverse waves have particle motion perpendicular to propagation (e.g., waves on a string), while longitudinal waves have particle motion parallel to propagation (e.g., sound waves). Mechanical waves require a material medium and transport energy through elastic coupling of medium constituents.
diagram_description: Comparison diagram showing transverse wave (particles moving up-down) and longitudinal wave (particles moving back-forth) with wave direction arrow
subject: physics

summary: A wave is a moving pattern of disturbance that carries energy and information from one location to another without the medium itself flowing. Unlike a stream (matter transport), waves involve oscillation of medium constituents about equilibrium. Examples include water waves, sound waves, and electromagnetic waves.
diagram_description: Pebble dropped in pond showing expanding circular ripples with cork pieces oscillating up-down to illustrate matter doesn't flow outward
subject: physics

summary: In transverse waves, particles of the medium oscillate perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation. They create shearing strain and can only propagate in solids and liquids with shear elasticity, not in gases. Examples: ripples on a string, electromagnetic waves.
diagram_description: Stretched string with transverse pulse showing particles moving up-down while wave travels left-right
subject: physics

summary: In longitudinal waves, particles oscillate parallel to the direction of propagation, creating alternating compressions and rarefactions. They propagate in all elastic media (solids, liquids, gases) because they depend on bulk modulus. Sound waves are the primary example of longitudinal mechanical waves.
diagram_description: Air column with piston showing compressed and rarefied regions moving along the pipe axis
subject: physics

summary: Sinusoidal travelling waves are described by y(x,t) = a sin(kx - ωt + φ), where each parameter has physical significance. This function satisfies the requirement that at any instant the wave has a definite spatial shape, and at any location particles execute simple harmonic motion. The phase determines displacement at any position and time.
diagram_description: Sinusoidal wave plot showing one complete wavelength with amplitude, crest, trough, and equilibrium labeled
subject: physics

summary: The displacement of a sinusoidal travelling wave is y(x,t) = a sin(kx - ωt + φ), where a is amplitude. At fixed time, this gives the spatial wave shape; at fixed position, it describes harmonic oscillation of that point. As time increases, the entire pattern shifts in the positive x direction with speed v = ω/k.
diagram_description: Multiple snapshots of a sinusoidal wave at different times showing rightward progression
formula: y(x, t) = A \sin(kx - \omega t) + B \cos(kx - \omega t)
subject: physics

summary: Amplitude a is the maximum displacement magnitude from equilibrium, always positive. The phase (kx - ωt + φ) is the argument of the sine function and determines displacement at any point and time. Initial phase φ is the phase at x = 0, t = 0 and can be set to zero by choosing suitable origin and time reference.
diagram_description: Sinusoidal wave showing amplitude as vertical distance from equilibrium to crest or trough
subject: physics

summary: Wavelength λ is the minimum distance between two points with the same phase (e.g., consecutive crests). Wave number k = 2π/λ is the angular propagation constant with SI units rad/m. Both quantities characterize the spatial periodicity of the wave pattern.
diagram_description: Sinusoidal wave with wavelength λ marked as distance between two consecutive crests
subject: physics

summary: Period T is the time for one complete oscillation at a fixed location. Frequency ν = 1/T is the number of oscillations per second (measured in hertz). Angular frequency ω = 2πν = 2π/T describes how fast the phase changes with time.
diagram_description: Time graph at fixed position showing one complete oscillation cycle with period T marked
subject: physics

summary: Wave speed v = ω/k = λν depends only on the elastic and inertial properties of the medium, not on frequency or wavelength. For mechanical waves, speed is determined by the ratio of elastic modulus to mass density. The wave equation v = λν relates wavelength and frequency for a given medium speed.
diagram_description: Two wave snapshots at times t and t + Δt showing how a crest moves distance Δx, illustrating wave speed
subject: physics

summary: The speed of transverse waves on a stretched string is v = √(T/μ), where T is tension and μ is linear mass density. This depends only on medium properties, not on the frequency of the wave. Increasing tension increases speed; increasing mass density decreases speed.
diagram_description: Stretched string under tension T with mass per unit length μ labeled, showing a transverse pulse
subject: physics

summary: The speed of longitudinal (sound) waves in a fluid is v = √(B/ρ), where B is bulk modulus and ρ is density. Solids and liquids have much higher sound speeds than gases because their bulk moduli are much larger, despite also having higher densities. Sound speed is independent of pressure for an ideal gas.
diagram_description: Table comparing sound speeds in various solids, liquids, and gases showing inverse relationship with bulk modulus
subject: physics

summary: For an ideal gas, Newton proposed v = √(P/ρ), where P is pressure. However, pressure variations in sound propagation are adiabatic, not isothermal, leading to a systematic 15% error. The corrected formula incorporates the adiabatic process condition.
diagram_description: Newton's formula derivation using ideal gas law with explanation of isothermal assumption limitation
subject: physics

summary: Laplace corrected Newton's formula by recognizing that pressure variations in sound waves are adiabatic (no time for heat exchange) rather than isothermal. The correct formula is v = √(γP/ρ), where γ = Cp/Cv is the specific heat ratio. For air at STP, this gives 331 m/s, matching experiments.
diagram_description: Comparison of Newton and Laplace formulas with calculated speeds at STP showing improvement with adiabatic correction
subject: physics

summary: When multiple waves pass through the same region, the net displacement is the algebraic sum of individual displacements (superposition principle). Depending on phase difference, waves can interfere constructively (maximum amplitude) or destructively (zero amplitude). This explains why water wave pulses pass through each other unchanged.
diagram_description: Two wave pulses of equal and opposite shape moving toward each other, showing intermediate state where they cancel
subject: physics

summary: When two or more waves traverse the same medium simultaneously, each acts independently as if others are absent. The net displacement at any point is the vector sum of displacements from individual waves. This principle holds because the wave equation is linear in displacement.
diagram_description: Multiple overlapping sinusoidal waves showing how individual displacements add to give net displacement at each point
subject: physics

summary: When two harmonic waves of equal amplitude and frequency interfere, constructive interference (φ = 0) produces maximum amplitude 2a, while destructive interference (φ = π) produces zero amplitude. Intermediate phase differences give intermediate amplitudes. This explains varying intensity in regions where waves overlap.
diagram_description: Two sinusoidal waves in phase and out of phase showing resultant with maximum and zero amplitude respectively
subject: physics

summary: For two harmonic waves with equal amplitude a and frequency but phase difference φ, the resultant wave has amplitude A(φ) = 2a cos(φ/2). This amplitude function varies from maximum 2a when φ = 0 to minimum 0 when φ = π. The phase relationship determines whether waves reinforce or cancel.
diagram_description: Graph of resultant amplitude versus phase difference φ, showing cosine variation from 2a to 0
subject: physics

subtopic_names: Normal Modes of Open and Closed Pipes
summary: When waves reflect at boundaries and interfere with incident waves, standing wave patterns form with fixed nodes (zero displacement) and antinodes (maximum displacement). The boundary conditions constrain possible wavelengths and frequencies to discrete normal modes. Strings and air columns vibrate at these resonant frequencies.
diagram_description: Standing wave pattern on a string showing nodes at fixed positions with antinodes in between, oscillating in time
subject: physics

summary: At a rigid (fixed) boundary, waves reflect with a phase reversal of π (180°), so the reflected wave is yr = -a sin(kx + ωt). At an open boundary, reflection occurs without phase change: yr = a sin(kx + ωt). The phase change arises from boundary conditions: zero displacement at rigid boundaries and maximum freedom at open boundaries.
diagram_description: Pulse meeting a rigid wall and bouncing back inverted, showing phase reversal; comparison with open boundary reflection
subject: physics

summary: A standing wave y(x,t) = 2a sin(kx) cos(ωt) arises from superposition of two identical waves travelling in opposite directions. Nodes (zero displacement) are fixed at positions x = nλ/2, separated by λ/2. Antinodes (maximum amplitude 2a) are fixed at x = (n + ½)λ/2. All points between nodes oscillate in phase with the same frequency but different amplitudes.
diagram_description: Standing wave showing fixed node and antinode positions throughout time, with particles oscillating vertically
subject: physics

summary: A string of length L fixed at both ends can vibrate only at discrete wavelengths λ = 2L/n (n = 1, 2, 3...) and corresponding frequencies νn = nv/(2L). The fundamental (n=1) has lowest frequency ν₁ = v/(2L); higher harmonics (n>1) are integer multiples. Musical instruments exploit this to produce specific pitches.
diagram_description: First six harmonics of a string fixed at both ends, showing progressively more nodes and antinodes
subject: physics

concept_name: Normal Modes of Open and Closed Pipes
summary: A pipe closed at one end and open at the other has a node at the closed end and antinode at the open end, giving frequencies νn = (n + ½)v/(2L) (n = 0, 1, 2...), containing only odd harmonics. A pipe open at both ends has antinodes at both ends and supports all harmonics νn = nv/(2L). Wind instruments use these resonances to amplify sound.
diagram_description: Standing wave patterns in closed-open pipe (odd harmonics only) and open-open pipe (all harmonics), showing node/antinode positions
subject: physics

summary: When two harmonic waves of nearly equal frequencies ν₁ and ν₂ are superposed, the resultant has constant average frequency but amplitude modulates at the beat frequency. The intensity waxes and wanes audibly, used by musicians to tune instruments until beat frequency vanishes. Beat frequency equals the difference of the two frequencies.
diagram_description: Superposition of two close-frequency sinusoids showing amplitude envelope (beat pattern) varying slowly with time
subject: physics

summary: When two sound waves of close frequencies interfere, the displacement is s = [2a cos(ωb t)] cos(ωa t), where ωb = (ω₁ - ω₂)/2 is the beat angular frequency. The term 2a cos(ωb t) acts as a slowly varying amplitude envelope. Musicians hear this as periodic loud-soft-loud intensity variation, useful for tuning.
diagram_description: Resultant sound wave from two close frequencies showing slow amplitude envelope (beat carrier) and fast oscillation
subject: physics

summary: The beat frequency is the rate at which sound intensity waxes and wanes, given by νbeat = |ν₁ - ν₂| or νbeat = |ω₁ - ω₂|/(2π). For two frequencies differing by 5 Hz, one hears 5 audible beats per second. This directly equals the frequency difference of the two interfering waves.
diagram_description: Time trace showing beat pattern with beat frequency equal to difference of the two input frequencies
subject: physics

summary: Wave propagation depends on elastic properties that provide restoring forces when the medium is disturbed. Bulk modulus B governs compression/expansion and determines longitudinal wave speed. Young's modulus Y governs extension/compression in bars. Shear modulus determines transverse wave speed. Higher moduli and lower density increase wave speed.
diagram_description: Diagrams showing how bulk modulus relates to volumetric compression and Young's modulus to linear strain
subject: physics

summary: Bulk modulus B = -ΔP/(ΔV/V) measures resistance to volume change under pressure. It determines the speed of longitudinal waves v = √(B/ρ). Solids and liquids have much larger bulk moduli than gases, explaining why sound travels faster in solids and liquids despite their greater density.
diagram_description: Block under uniform pressure change showing volumetric compression and definition of bulk modulus
subject: physics

summary: Young's modulus Y = stress/strain measures resistance to stretching or compressing a bar along its length. The speed of longitudinal waves in a solid bar is v = √(Y/ρ). Young's modulus has the same dimension as bulk modulus but applies to one-dimensional deformation.
diagram_description: Bar under tensile stress showing elongation and definition of Young's modulus as stress-to-strain ratio
subject: physics

summary: Shear modulus (or rigidity) measures resistance to shape change without volume change. Only media with nonzero shear modulus (solids) can sustain transverse waves; gases and liquids cannot. The existence of shear modulus in solids enables propagation of both longitudinal and transverse waves.
diagram_description: Rectangular block under shear stress showing angular deformation and relationship to shear modulus
subject: physics

summary: Wave motion was first scientifically analyzed in the 17th century by Christiaan Huygens (1629-1695), Robert Hooke, and Isaac Newton. These scientists built understanding by analogy with oscillating masses on springs and pendulums. Modern wave theory emerged from recognizing the deep connection between oscillatory and wave phenomena in elastic media.
subject: physics

summary: Water surface waves are of two types: capillary waves (wavelength < few cm, restoring force = surface tension) and gravity waves (wavelength several metres to hundreds, restoring force = gravity). Ocean waves combine both longitudinal and transverse motion and involve complicated particle trajectories extending to the bottom. The relative importance of capillary and gravity forces depends on wavelength.
diagram_description: Water surface showing small ripples (capillary) and large ocean waves (gravity), with particle motion patterns
subject: physics

summary: Electric charge is a fundamental property of matter that causes it to experience a force in an electromagnetic field. There are two types: positive and negative, with like charges repelling and unlike charges attracting. Charge is quantised (q = ne) and conserved in isolated systems. The SI unit is the coulomb (C).
diagram_description: Diagram showing two glass rods repelling, two plastic rods repelling, and a glass rod attracting a plastic rod
subject: physics

summary: By convention, charge on a glass rod rubbed with silk is positive; charge on a plastic rod rubbed with fur is negative. Like charges repel each other, and unlike charges attract. Polarity is the property that differentiates the two types of charges.
diagram_description: Side-by-side diagrams of two positive charges repelling and one positive and one negative charge attracting with force vectors
subject: physics

summary: All free charges are integral multiples of the elementary charge e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C. Any charge q on a body is given by q = ne, where n is an integer. This quantisation is imperceptible at macroscopic scales where charges are enormous compared to e.
diagram_description: Number line showing discrete charge values as multiples of elementary charge e
subject: physics

summary: The total charge of a system is the algebraic sum of all individual charges, treating charges like real numbers with proper signs. For a system with charges q₁, q₂, ..., qₙ, total charge Q = q₁ + q₂ + ... + qₙ. Positive and negative charges can cancel.
subject: physics

summary: The total charge of an isolated system remains constant with time. When bodies are charged through friction, charge transfers from one to the other, but no charge is created or destroyed. Experimentally verified and holds in all interactions.
subject: physics

summary: Conductors (metals, earth, human body) allow free movement of electric charges through them. Insulators (glass, plastic, wood, nylon) resist charge flow. In conductors, charge redistributes freely over the surface; in insulators, charge remains localized.
diagram_description: Diagram comparing charge distribution on a conducting sphere versus an insulating sphere
subject: physics

summary: Conductors are materials with comparatively free-moving electric charges (electrons in metals, ions in electrolytes). They allow electricity to pass through them easily. Examples include metals, human body, and earth.
subject: physics

summary: Insulators are materials with charges tightly bound to atoms, offering high resistance to electric current flow. Examples include glass, porcelain, plastic, nylon, and wood. Charge introduced to an insulator remains at that location.
subject: physics

summary: When charge is transferred to a conductor, it redistributes instantly over the entire surface. Inside a conductor, the electric field is zero in electrostatic equilibrium. This property is fundamental to understanding shielding and grounding.
diagram_description: Diagram showing charge distributed uniformly on the surface of a conducting sphere with zero field inside
subject: physics

summary: Coulomb's law states that the electrostatic force between two point charges is directly proportional to the product of charges and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them. The constant of proportionality in SI units is k ≈ 9 × 10⁹ N m² C⁻². Force is repulsive for like charges and attractive for unlike charges.
diagram_description: Two point charges separated by distance r with force vectors showing attraction or repulsion
subject: physics

summary: F = (1/4πε₀) × (q₁q₂/r²), where k = 1/4πε₀ ≈ 9 × 10⁹ N m² C⁻². The constant ε₀ (permittivity of free space) is 8.854 × 10⁻¹² C² N⁻¹ m⁻². Coulomb defined the unit of charge such that the force between 1 C charges 1 m apart equals 9 × 10⁹ N.
subject: physics

summary: The force on charge q₁ due to q₂ is **F₁₂** = (1/4πε₀) × (q₁q₂/r₁₂²) × **r̂₂₁**, where **r̂₂₁** is the unit vector from q₁ to q₂. This form automatically gives the correct direction: repulsive for like charges and attractive for unlike charges.
diagram_description: Vector diagram showing two charges with position vectors and force vector between them
subject: physics

summary: The net force on a charge due to multiple other charges is the vector sum of forces due to each charge individually. Each pairwise force is unaffected by the presence of other charges; there are no three-body or many-body forces in electrostatics. This principle enables calculation of fields and forces for complex charge distributions.
diagram_description: Three charges with force vectors from each on a central test charge, showing vector addition
subject: physics

subtopic_names: Electric Field Due to Point Charge
Electric Field Due to System of Charges
summary: Electric field **E** at a point is the electric force per unit positive test charge placed at that point: **E** = **F**/q. It is a vector quantity independent of the test charge and characterizes the electrical environment created by source charges. SI unit is N/C or V/m.
diagram_description: Diagram showing electric field vectors pointing radially outward from a positive charge
subject: physics

summary: Electric field is defined operationally as **E** = lim(q→0) **F**/q, independent of test charge q. For a positive source charge, field points radially outward; for negative, radially inward. Field is independent of test charge and depends only on source charges and position.
diagram_description: Two diagrams: field lines radiating outward from positive charge and inward toward negative charge
subject: physics

concept_name: Electric Field Due to Point Charge
summary: The electric field at distance r from a point charge q is **E** = (1/4πε₀) × (q/r²) **r̂**, where **r̂** is the radial unit vector. Magnitude depends only on distance r; direction is radially outward for q > 0 and radially inward for q < 0. Field has spherical symmetry.
diagram_description: Radial field pattern around a positive point charge with field line density decreasing with distance
subject: physics

concept_name: Electric Field Due to System of Charges
summary: The electric field at a point due to multiple charges is the vector sum of fields due to each charge: **E** = Σ **Eᵢ** = (1/4πε₀) Σ (qᵢ/rᵢ²) **r̂ᵢ**. This applies both to discrete charge systems and continuous distributions (via integration).
diagram_description: Three point charges with field vectors from each, showing vector addition at a test point

builds_upon.concept_name: Electric Field Due to Point Charge

subject: physics

summary: An electric field line is a curve whose tangent at each point gives the direction of **E** at that point. Field line density (closeness) indicates field strength: crowded where field is strong, sparse where weak. Lines start at positive charges and end at negative charges; they never cross or form closed loops.
diagram_description: Field line patterns for: single positive charge (radial), single negative charge (radial inward), dipole, and two like charges
subject: physics

summary: An electric dipole is a pair of equal and opposite charges +q and −q separated by distance 2a. The dipole moment **p** = q(2a)**p̂** points from negative to positive charge. Dipoles are fundamental in explaining polarization, molecular behavior, and interactions with external fields.
diagram_description: Diagram showing two opposite charges separated by 2a with dipole moment vector pointing from −q to +q
subject: physics

summary: Dipole moment **p** is a vector quantity with magnitude p = q × 2a and direction from negative to positive charge. It characterizes the strength and orientation of a dipole. Unit: coulomb-meter (C·m). The dipole field at large distances depends on **p**, not on q and a separately.
subject: physics

summary: On the dipole axis, **E** = (1/4πε₀) × (2p/r³) **p̂**; on the equatorial plane, **E** = −(1/4πε₀) × (p/r³) **p̂**. Unlike point charge field (∝ 1/r²), dipole field falls off as 1/r³ because total charge is zero. Field magnitude and direction depend on both distance r and angle θ.
diagram_description: Field line pattern of a dipole showing stronger field on axis pointing along dipole moment and weaker field in equatorial plane

builds_upon.concept_name: Electric Field Due to Point Charge

subject: physics

summary: In a uniform external field **E**, a dipole experiences torque **τ** = **p** × **E** but no net force. The torque tends to align the dipole with the field. In a non-uniform field, the dipole also experiences a net force along the field gradient if **p** is parallel to **E**.
diagram_description: Dipole in uniform field showing torque causing rotation, and dipole in non-uniform field showing net force
subject: physics

summary: Electric flux Φ through an area element is Φ = **E** · **ΔS** = E ΔS cos θ, proportional to the number of field lines crossing the surface. Total flux through a closed surface is Φ = Q_enclosed/ε₀ (Gauss's law). Flux is independent of the shape of the surface.
diagram_description: Area element with field lines passing through it at angle θ to the normal
subject: physics

summary: Electric flux through an area element **ΔS** is defined as Φ = **E** · **ΔS**, where **ΔS** = ΔS **n̂** (area times outward normal unit vector). For a closed surface, total flux is Φ = ∮ **E** · d**S**. Flux is positive when **E** has a component out of the surface.
diagram_description: Tilted area element with field vector and normal vector showing angle θ between them
subject: physics

summary: Gauss's law states: electric flux through any closed surface is Φ = Q_enclosed/ε₀, where Q_enclosed is the total charge inside. It is a consequence of Coulomb's law and the superposition principle. Useful for calculating fields when charge distribution has symmetry (spherical, cylindrical, planar).
diagram_description: Closed Gaussian surface enclosing a charge with field lines passing through
subject: physics

subtopic_names: Field Due to Infinite Plane Sheet
summary: Gauss's law simplifies field calculations for symmetric charge configurations. For an infinite uniformly charged wire: E = λ/(2πε₀r). For an infinite plane sheet: E = σ/(2ε₀). For a uniformly charged spherical shell: E = q/(4πε₀r²) outside, E = 0 inside.
diagram_description: Three diagrams showing Gaussian surfaces for wire (cylinder), plane (box), and sphere with field patterns
subject: physics

summary: For an infinitely long straight wire with uniform linear charge density λ, the electric field at perpendicular distance r is **E** = (λ/2πε₀r) **n̂**, where **n̂** is the radial unit vector. Field is radially outward for λ > 0, inward for λ < 0. Magnitude depends only on perpendicular distance.
diagram_description: Infinite wire with concentric circles showing field vectors radially outward
subject: physics

concept_name: Field Due to Infinite Plane Sheet
summary: For an infinite plane sheet with uniform surface charge density σ, the electric field is **E** = (σ/2ε₀) **n̂** on both sides, where **n̂** is normal to the plane. Field is uniform, perpendicular to the sheet, and independent of distance from the sheet. Direction is away from sheet for σ > 0.
diagram_description: Infinite plane sheet with parallel field lines extending uniformly on both sides
subject: physics

summary: A uniformly charged thin spherical shell of radius R with total charge q produces: outside (r > R): **E** = q/(4πε₀r²) **r̂** (as if charge were at center); inside (r < R): **E** = 0. This is the shell theorem—a key result confirming the 1/r² dependence in Coulomb's law.
diagram_description: Spherical shell with field lines pointing radially outward outside, and zero field inside
subject: physics

summary: For macroscopic charge distributions, discrete charges are replaced by densities: linear λ = ΔQ/Δl (C/m), surface σ = ΔQ/ΔS (C/m²), volume ρ = ΔQ/ΔV (C/m³). These ignore microscopic discreteness and quantisation, treating charge as continuous. Electric field is then found by integration over the distribution.
diagram_description: Three diagrams showing small elements of line charge, surface charge, and volume charge with densities labeled
subject: physics

summary: Linear charge density λ is the charge per unit length of a thin wire or line: λ = ΔQ/Δl with units C/m. For a uniformly charged wire, λ is constant. Total charge on a wire segment of length L is Q = λL. Used to calculate fields from charged wires using integration.
subject: physics

summary: Surface charge density σ is the charge per unit area on a surface: σ = ΔQ/ΔS with units C/m². For a uniformly charged surface, σ is constant. Total charge on a surface area A is Q = σA. Used for charged conductors, plates, and shells.
subject: physics

summary: Volume charge density ρ is the charge per unit volume in a region: ρ = ΔQ/ΔV with units C/m³. For uniform distribution, ρ is constant. Total charge in volume V is Q = ρV. Used for uniformly charged spheres, cylinders, and bulk materials.
subject: physics

concept_name: Comparison of Electric and Gravitational Forces
summary: The ratio of electric force to gravitational force between a proton and electron is approximately 2.4 × 10³⁹. Electric force is enormously stronger, yet gravity dominates at cosmic scales because it is always attractive while electric forces can cancel. This ratio shows why gravity is weak compared to electromagnetism.
subject: physics

summary: An electroscope is a device for detecting electric charge. It consists of a vertical metal rod with thin gold leaves attached to the bottom end in a box. When a charged object touches the metal knob, charge flows to the leaves causing them to diverge. The degree of divergence indicates charge magnitude.
diagram_description: Gold-leaf electroscope with metal knob at top and two gold leaves at bottom in a glass box
subject: physics

summary: When a charged object approaches an insulator (e.g., a comb near paper), it creates a non-uniform electric field that induces a dipole moment in the neutral insulator. The dipole experiences a net force toward the region of stronger field, causing attraction even though the insulator is uncharged. This explains why charged combs attract uncharged paper pieces.
diagram_description: Charged comb near neutral paper with induced dipoles in paper molecules and net attractive force
subject: physics

summary: Polar molecules have permanent electric dipole moments because their centres of positive and negative charge do not coincide (e.g., H₂O). Non-polar molecules like CO₂ and CH₄ have zero dipole moment but develop one in an external field. Molecular dipoles explain intermolecular forces and material properties.
diagram_description: Water molecule showing oxygen and hydrogen atoms with displaced positive and negative charge centres
subject: physics

subtopic_names: Potential Due to System of Charges
Relation Between Electric Field and Potential
summary: Electrostatic potential at a point is the work done by an external force per unit positive charge in bringing it from infinity to that point. Potential is a scalar quantity and is path-independent due to the conservative nature of the electrostatic force. Potential difference between two points is physically significant, though absolute potential is arbitrary to within a constant.
subject: physics

summary: Electric potential V at a point is defined as the work done by an external force in bringing a unit positive charge from infinity to that point without acceleration. It is given by V = W/q, where W is work done and q is the test charge. Potential at infinity is conventionally taken as zero.
diagram_description: Diagram showing a unit positive charge being brought from infinity to a point P in an electric field with work W labeled
subject: physics

summary: The electric potential at distance r from a point charge Q is V(r) = Q/(4πε₀r). This potential is positive for positive charges and negative for negative charges, falling off as 1/r. Potential is continuous everywhere except at the location of the charge.
diagram_description: Concentric spheres around a point charge showing equipotential surfaces with potential values labeled

builds_upon.concept_name: Electric Field Due to Point Charge

subject: physics

summary: The electric potential due to a dipole at distance r (r >> a) is V = (p·r̂)/(4πε₀r²), where p is the dipole moment. Unlike point charge potential (∝ 1/r), dipole potential falls off as 1/r² and depends on angle θ between position vector and dipole moment. On the dipole axis potential is ±p/(4πε₀r²); in the equatorial plane it is zero.
diagram_description: Electric dipole with position vector r at angle θ showing equipotential surfaces for dipole configuration
subject: physics

concept_name: Potential Due to System of Charges
summary: The total electric potential at a point due to multiple charges is the algebraic sum of potentials due to individual charges: V = V₁ + V₂ + ... + Vₙ. This follows from the superposition principle and applies whether charges are positive or negative. Sign of each charge is automatically included in its contribution.
diagram_description: Multiple point charges with different signs showing the vector summation of potential contributions at a field point

builds_upon.concept_name: Electric Field Due to System of Charges

subject: physics

summary: An equipotential surface is a surface at all points of which the electric potential has the same constant value. For a point charge, equipotential surfaces are concentric spheres; for a uniform field, they are parallel planes perpendicular to the field direction. No work is done in moving a charge on an equipotential surface.
diagram_description: Equipotential surfaces drawn as concentric spheres around a point charge or parallel planes in a uniform field
subject: physics

concept_name: Relation Between Electric Field and Potential
summary: The electric field is related to potential by E = -∇V or E = -dV/dl, where l is perpendicular to the equipotential surface. Electric field points in the direction of steepest decrease of potential and is always perpendicular to equipotential surfaces. Magnitude equals potential change per unit distance normal to equipotential.
diagram_description: Two adjacent equipotential surfaces with field lines perpendicular to them, showing potential gradient direction
subject: physics

subtopic_names: Electrostatic Potential Energy of Single Charge
Potential Energy of System of Charges
Potential Energy of Dipole in External Field
summary: Potential energy in electrostatics is the work required to assemble a charge configuration against the electrostatic force. Since the electrostatic force is conservative, potential energy depends only on final configuration, not the path taken. Work done by external force equals the change in potential energy.
subject: physics

concept_name: Electrostatic Potential Energy of Single Charge
summary: The potential energy of a charge q in an external electric field is U = qV(r), where V(r) is the external potential at position r. This represents the work required to bring the charge from infinity to that point. The energy is positive for positive charges in positive potential regions and negative for unlike sign combinations.
diagram_description: A charge q being brought from infinity to a point in an electric field with potential V marked
subject: physics

concept_name: Potential Energy of System of Charges
summary: The potential energy of a system of charges is the total work required to assemble all charges at their specified locations, brought from infinity. For two charges: U = q₁q₂/(4πε₀r₁₂). For multiple charges, sum all pairwise interactions. Total energy is independent of assembly sequence due to conservation principle.
diagram_description: Multiple point charges at their final positions showing distances between pairs, with assembly sequence illustrated

builds_upon.concept_name: Electrostatic Potential Energy of Single Charge
Potential Due to System of Charges

subject: physics

concept_name: Potential Energy of Dipole in External Field
summary: A dipole with moment p in uniform external field E has potential energy U = -p·E = -pE cos θ, where θ is angle between p and E. Energy is minimum (-pE) when dipole aligns with field and maximum (+pE) when antiparallel. The dipole experiences torque τ = p × E tending to align it with the field.
diagram_description: Electric dipole at various angles θ to the field direction, showing torque and potential energy variation graph

builds_upon.concept_name: Electrostatic Potential Energy of Single Charge

subject: physics

summary: When a system of charges is placed in an external electric field, total potential energy includes both mutual interaction energy and interaction with the external field. For charge q at position r: U_external = qV_external(r). For two charges: U_total = U_mutual + q₁V(r₁) + q₂V(r₂).
diagram_description: Two charges in an external uniform field showing both pairwise interaction and field interaction contributions

builds_upon.concept_name: Electrostatic Potential Energy of Single Charge

subject: physics

summary: In electrostatics, conductors exhibit unique properties: electric field is zero in the interior, all excess charge resides on the surface, and the conductor body is at constant potential. These properties result from free charge carriers that redistribute to cancel any internal field.
subject: physics

summary: In electrostatic equilibrium, the electric field inside a conductor is always zero. Free charge carriers respond to any internal field and redistribute until their motion ceases, which occurs only when the internal field vanishes completely. This is the defining property of a conductor.
diagram_description: Cross-section of a conductor showing field lines terminating at surface with zero field indicated inside
subject: physics

summary: All excess charge on an isolated conductor must reside on its surface; the interior carries zero net charge in electrostatic equilibrium. This follows from Gauss's law applied to any closed surface inside the conductor. Charge distributes on the surface such that the interior field remains zero.
diagram_description: Conductor with charge distribution shown as dots on surface only, Gaussian surface drawn inside showing no flux
subject: physics

summary: At the surface of a charged conductor, the electric field is always perpendicular (normal) to the surface with magnitude E = σ/ε₀, where σ is the local surface charge density. Field cannot have a tangential component because charge on the surface would experience force and move. Field is discontinuous across the surface, jumping from zero inside to σ/ε₀ outside.
diagram_description: Conductor surface with pill-box Gaussian surface showing field discontinuity and normal field direction
subject: physics

summary: A conductor in electrostatic equilibrium is an equipotential body: all points inside and on the surface have the same electric potential. This follows because E = 0 inside, so no work is done moving a test charge within or on the conductor. For a charged isolated conductor, potential depends on total charge and geometry.
diagram_description: Conductor showing equipotential surfaces coinciding with its boundary and interior marked V = constant
subject: physics

summary: A cavity inside a conductor is shielded from external electric fields: the field inside a charge-free cavity is always zero, regardless of the conductor's charge or external field configuration. This occurs because induced charges on the cavity surface create a field that exactly cancels any external influence. Effect is used to protect sensitive instruments from external electrical interference.
diagram_description: Conductor with cavity showing external charges/field and zero field marked inside the charge-free cavity
subject: physics

summary: Dielectrics are insulating materials that become polarized in an external electric field. The external field induces or aligns dipole moments in molecules, creating a net polarization P that produces an internal field opposing the applied field. This reduces the net field inside the dielectric and increases capacitor capacitance.
subject: physics

summary: Dielectrics are non-conducting materials with no free charge carriers, unlike conductors. They contain only bound charges within atoms and molecules. When placed in an external electric field, dielectrics respond by developing net dipole moment without allowing charge to flow freely across the material.
diagram_description: Atoms/molecules in a dielectric before and after applying external field showing bound charge separation
subject: physics

summary: Polarization P is the dipole moment per unit volume of a dielectric. In an external field, non-polar molecules develop induced dipoles through charge displacement, while polar molecules align their permanent dipoles with the field. Both mechanisms contribute to net polarization in the direction of the applied field.
diagram_description: Non-polar molecules developing induced dipoles and polar molecules aligning in external field
subject: physics

summary: Non-polar molecules (like O₂, H₂) have no permanent dipole moment but develop induced dipoles when placed in an external field through charge separation. Polar molecules (like HCl, H₂O) possess permanent dipoles even without external field; the field tends to align these dipoles. Both types contribute to dielectric polarization.
diagram_description: Non-polar and polar molecules shown before and after external field application with dipole moments indicated
subject: physics

summary: Electric susceptibility χₑ is a dimensionless material constant that relates polarization to applied field: P = ε₀χₑE. It is always positive and measures the material's tendency to polarize. Different materials have different susceptibilities; higher values indicate stronger polarization response.
diagram_description: Table or graph showing electric susceptibility values for different dielectric materials
subject: physics

summary: Permittivity ε = ε₀K is the product of vacuum permittivity ε₀ and dimensionless dielectric constant K (K > 1). The dielectric constant K = ε/ε₀ = C/C₀ is the ratio of capacitance with dielectric to capacitance in vacuum. It measures the degree to which a material reduces the electric field compared to vacuum.
diagram_description: Comparison of field strength with and without dielectric, showing field reduction by factor K
subject: physics

summary: A capacitor is a system of two conductors (usually plates) separated by an insulator that stores electric charge. Capacitance C = Q/V is the ratio of charge to potential difference between conductors. Capacitance depends only on geometry (area, separation) and dielectric material, not on Q or V. Unit is farad (F).
subject: physics

summary: Capacitance C is defined as the ratio of charge Q on one conductor to the potential difference V between the two conductors: C = Q/V. It is an intrinsic property of the capacitor geometry and dielectric, remaining constant regardless of the applied voltage. SI unit is farad (F), where 1 F = 1 C/V.
diagram_description: Two conductors with charges +Q and -Q, separated by potential difference V, with C = Q/V labeled
subject: physics

summary: A parallel plate capacitor consists of two large parallel conducting plates of area A separated by small distance d. For vacuum between plates, capacitance is C = ε₀A/d. The electric field between plates is uniform (E = σ/ε₀ = Q/(ε₀A)) and confined to the region between plates; fringing effects at edges are negligible when d² << A.
diagram_description: Two parallel plates with area A, separation d, uniform field lines between them, and capacitance formula labeled

builds_upon.concept_name: Field Due to Infinite Plane Sheet

subject: physics

summary: When a dielectric of constant K is inserted between capacitor plates, the capacitance increases to C = KC₀ = Kε₀A/d. The dielectric polarizes, producing an internal field that reduces the net field inside by factor K. This increases the capacitance by the same factor, allowing storage of more charge at lower voltage without breakdown.
diagram_description: Parallel plate capacitor with dielectric slab inserted between plates, showing polarization and field reduction
subject: physics

summary: Capacitors connected in series have equal charge Q but different voltages; equivalent capacitance is 1/C_eq = 1/C₁ + 1/C₂ + ... Capacitors in parallel have equal voltage but different charges; equivalent capacitance is C_eq = C₁ + C₂ + ... Series combination gives smaller capacitance; parallel gives larger.
diagram_description: Circuit diagrams showing two capacitors in series and in parallel with voltage/charge distribution labeled
subject: physics

summary: Energy stored in a charged capacitor is U = ½QV = ½CV² = Q²/(2C), representing work done in charging. Energy is stored in the electric field; energy density u = ½ε₀E² per unit volume is uniform between plates. When two capacitors are connected, some energy is lost as heat if they have different initial charges.
diagram_description: Charged capacitor with energy U labeled, showing field lines and energy density distribution between plates

builds_upon.concept_name: Electrostatic Potential Energy of Single Charge

subject: physics

summary: The electron volt (eV) is a unit of energy equal to the work done on an electron accelerated through potential difference of 1 volt: 1 eV = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ J. Commonly used in atomic and nuclear physics. Subunits include keV (10³ eV), MeV (10⁶ eV), GeV (10⁹ eV), TeV (10¹² eV).
subject: physics

summary: An electric dipole in a uniform external field experiences torque τ = p × E = pE sin θ, where θ is the angle between dipole moment and field. Torque tends to align the dipole with the field. Torque is maximum (pE) when dipole is perpendicular to field and zero when parallel or antiparallel.
diagram_description: Dipole at various angles to external field showing torque vector and magnitude variation
subject: physics

summary: Near the edges of a parallel plate capacitor, electric field lines bend outward instead of remaining perfectly perpendicular between the plates. This fringing effect means the field is not uniformly E = σ/ε₀ at the edges. Effect is negligible when plate separation d² << plate area A, allowing uniform field approximation in the central region.
diagram_description: Electric field lines between parallel plates showing bending at the edges (fringing effect)
subject: physics

summary: Electric current is the net charge flowing per unit time across a cross-section of a conductor. For steady current, I = q/t where q is charge and t is time. More generally, instantaneous current is the limit of ΔQ/Δt as Δt→0, measured in amperes (A). Current direction is defined as the flow of positive charge.
diagram_description: Cross-section of conductor with positive and negative charge carriers moving in opposite directions across an area element
subject: physics

summary: Current is defined as the ratio I = q/t for steady current, and as dQ/dt for time-varying current. The SI unit is the ampere (A), equal to one coulomb per second. Current can be positive (forward) or negative (backward) depending on reference direction.
subject: physics

summary: In conductors, current arises from the flow of both positive and negative charges. The net current is the difference between positive charge flowing forward and negative charge flowing forward (or equivalently, positive charge flowing backward). Both types of charge carriers contribute to total current in electrolytes and semiconductors.
diagram_description: Two charge carriers moving in opposite directions across a conductor cross-section
subject: physics

summary: Steady current is proportional to time interval, allowing use of I = q/t. Time-varying current requires the instantaneous definition I(t) = lim(ΔQ/Δt) as Δt→0. Lightning is an example of highly time-varying current; domestic devices use steady current.
diagram_description: Graph of current versus time showing constant steady current versus fluctuating time-varying current
subject: physics

summary: Ohm's law states that voltage is proportional to current: V = IR. Resistance R is a material property dependent on length and cross-section via R = ρl/A, where ρ is resistivity. Resistance increases with temperature in metals but decreases in semiconductors.
diagram_description: V-I characteristic curve showing linear relationship for an ohmic conductor
subject: physics

summary: Ohm's law states V ∝ I, or V = RI, where R is the resistance (constant for ohmic conductors). Discovered in 1828 by G.S. Ohm, it holds for metals and many other conductors over a limited range of fields. The law expresses proportionality between driving voltage and resulting current.
diagram_description: Circuit diagram with battery, resistor, and labelled voltage V across resistor carrying current I
subject: physics

summary: Resistance R = ρl/A depends on material resistivity ρ, length l, and area A. Doubling length doubles resistance; halving area doubles resistance. Resistivity is independent of shape and depends only on material. The SI unit of resistance is the ohm (Ω).
diagram_description: Rectangular conductor slab showing length l, cross-sectional area A, with resistance R indicated
subject: physics

summary: For metals, resistivity increases linearly with temperature: ρ_T = ρ₀[1 + α(T − T₀)], where α is the temperature coefficient (positive for metals). Semiconductors show opposite behaviour: resistivity decreases with increasing temperature. Nichrome and constantan have weak temperature dependence and are used in standard resistors.
diagram_description: Graph of resistivity ρ_T versus temperature T for copper showing linear increase, and for a semiconductor showing nonlinear decrease
subject: physics

summary: In metals, current is carried by free electrons that drift slowly (mm/s) under applied electric field despite high random thermal speeds. Electrons collide frequently with fixed ions, losing random motion components but gaining average drift velocity opposite to the field. Drift velocity produces current despite numerous collisions.
diagram_description: Electron trajectory showing random zigzag motion between collisions with slight net drift superimposed
subject: physics

summary: In conductors, some electrons are free to move within the material (especially in metals). Without an applied field, electrons undergo random thermal motion and collide with fixed ions, emerging with the same speed but random direction. The average velocity is zero, producing no net current. When a field is applied, electrons accelerate between collisions.
diagram_description: Crystal lattice with electrons showing random thermal motion vectors and collision points with fixed ions
subject: physics

summary: Drift velocity v_d is the average velocity of charge carriers in response to an applied electric field. Derived as v_d = −(eEτ)/m for electrons, where τ is the average relaxation time (time between collisions). Drift velocity is typically very small (mm/s) despite thermal speeds of ~10⁵ m/s, and is independent of time despite constant acceleration.
diagram_description: Electron path showing acceleration from E-field punctuated by collisions, with small net drift superimposed on large random motion
subject: physics

summary: Current density j = nev_d gives charge per unit time per unit area perpendicular to flow, where n is carrier density and e is charge magnitude. Mobility μ = |v_d|/E relates drift velocity to applied field. Conductivity σ = neμ = ne²τ/m connects macroscopic current to microscopic electron parameters.
diagram_description: Conductor cross-section showing charge carrier distribution per unit volume and drift velocity vectors
subject: physics

summary: A cell maintains steady current by converting chemical energy to electrical energy. The electromotive force (EMF) ε is the work per unit charge done by the cell's internal mechanism, equal to the potential difference between terminals in open circuit. The cell's internal resistance r reduces the terminal voltage when current flows: V = ε − Ir.
diagram_description: Cross-section of electrolytic cell showing positive and negative electrodes in electrolyte, with terminals P and N marked
subject: physics

summary: EMF ε is the work per unit charge done by non-conservative forces inside the cell. In open circuit (I = 0), EMF equals the potential difference between terminals: ε = V(+) − V(−). When current flows, terminal voltage V = ε − Ir drops below EMF due to internal resistance. EMF is not a force but a voltage.
diagram_description: Cell circuit diagram showing emf ε, internal resistance r, external resistance R, with voltage labels
subject: physics

summary: Internal resistance r is the resistance of the electrolyte and electrode materials inside a cell. It causes voltage drop Ir when current flows, reducing terminal voltage below EMF. The maximum current a cell can supply is I_max = ε/r. Dry cells have higher internal resistance than electrolytic cells.
diagram_description: Cell equivalent circuit showing emf ε in series with internal resistance r
subject: physics

summary: For n cells in series (positive of one to negative of next): ε_eq = ε₁ + ε₂ + ... and r_eq = r₁ + r₂ + .... For n identical cells in parallel (all positives and negatives connected): ε_eq = ε and 1/r_eq = 1/r₁ + 1/r₂ + .... Series increases voltage; parallel increases current capacity without changing voltage.
diagram_description: Two diagrams: one showing three cells in series with emfs and internal resistances; another showing three cells in parallel
subject: physics

summary: Power dissipated in a resistor is P = IV = I²R = V²/R. Energy is dissipated as heat when charges lose kinetic energy in collisions with ions. In transmission lines, power loss P_loss = I²R_c is reduced by using high voltage, since P_loss ∝ 1/V². Transformers step voltage up for transmission, down for use.
diagram_description: Circuit showing resistor with voltage V and current I, with power dissipated indicated
subject: physics

summary: Power dissipated in a resistor R carrying current I is P = I²R, or equivalently P = V²/R where V is the voltage across R. This power comes from the source's EMF and is converted to heat. Higher power dissipation occurs at higher currents or higher resistances.
diagram_description: Resistor in circuit showing current I and voltage V with power P label
subject: physics

summary: Joule heating arises because moving electrons lose kinetic energy in collisions with ions. Energy gained from the electric field is transferred to ions, causing them to vibrate more vigorously. Total energy dissipated as heat in time Δt is ΔW = IVΔt. This mechanism explains why conductors heat up and resistor filaments incandescence.
diagram_description: Electron collision with fixed ion showing kinetic energy conversion to thermal vibration
subject: physics

summary: Power loss in transmission wires is P_loss = I²R_c ∝ V⁻². Transmission at high voltage and low current minimizes loss for a given power P. Transformers step voltage up (increases at power station) for transmission, then down (at user end) to safe levels. This is why long-distance power lines use thousands of volts.
diagram_description: Power transmission system showing power station, high-voltage transmission lines, step-up transformer, step-down transformer, and users
subject: physics

summary: Kirchhoff's rules allow analysis of complex circuits. The junction rule (conservation of charge) states that current entering a junction equals current leaving. The loop rule states that the algebraic sum of potential changes around a closed loop is zero. Together they enable solving for all currents and voltages in multi-loop circuits.
diagram_description: Multi-loop circuit with junctions and loops labelled, showing current directions and voltage polarities
subject: physics

summary: Kirchhoff's junction rule states: at any junction, the sum of currents entering equals the sum leaving (ΣI_in = ΣI_out). This follows from charge conservation—in steady state, charge cannot accumulate at a point. The rule applies to any point in a line or junction of multiple branches.
diagram_description: Junction with three branches showing currents I₁, I₂, I₃ entering and leaving with directional arrows
subject: physics

summary: Kirchhoff's loop rule states: the algebraic sum of changes in potential around any closed loop is zero (Σ ΔV = 0). This follows because electric potential depends only on position—starting and ending at the same point requires zero net potential change. Applied to resistors and cells in a loop, it relates currents to voltages.
diagram_description: Closed loop in circuit with resistors and a cell, showing voltage rises and drops around the loop
subject: physics

summary: A Wheatstone bridge consists of four resistors R₁, R₂, R₃, R₄ connected in a diamond with a galvanometer across one diagonal and a battery across the other. At balance (null deflection), R₁/R₂ = R₃/R₄. This null-point condition allows accurate measurement of an unknown resistance without precise ammeter readings.
diagram_description: Diamond circuit diagram with four resistors at vertices, battery across one diagonal, galvanometer across the other
subject: physics

summary: Materials are classified by resistivity: metals (conductors) have ρ ≈ 10⁻⁸–10⁻⁶ Ωm; insulators have ρ ≈ 10¹⁸–10²⁴ Ωm; semiconductors lie between these on a logarithmic scale. Conductors have many free electrons; insulators have electrons tightly bound; semiconductors have few free carriers but can be doped to increase conductivity.
diagram_description: Table or logarithmic scale showing resistivity ranges for conductors, semiconductors, and insulators
subject: physics

summary: Conductors (e.g. copper, silver, aluminum) have resistivity in the range 10⁻⁸–10⁻⁶ Ωm. In metals, some electrons are practically free to move within the bulk material. This large number of mobile charge carriers (n ≈ 10²⁸ m⁻³) allows efficient current flow. Resistivity increases with temperature due to increased collision frequency.
subject: physics

summary: Semiconductors (e.g. silicon, germanium) have intermediate resistivity that decreases with increasing temperature—opposite to metals. Adding small amounts of dopant impurities dramatically increases conductivity. This temperature dependence and tunability make semiconductors essential for electronic devices like diodes and transistors.
subject: physics

summary: Insulators (e.g. rubber, ceramic, glass, plastic) have very high resistivity (10¹⁸–10²⁴ Ωm), roughly 10²² times greater than metals. Electrons are tightly bound to atoms and do not accelerate significantly even under applied electric field. They prevent unwanted current flow in circuits and are essential for safety.
subject: physics

summary: Many materials deviate from Ohm's law in three ways: (1) V is non-proportional to I (V-I curve is non-linear); (2) V-I relation is asymmetric in voltage sign (diode rectification); (3) V-I relation is non-unique (GaAs). These non-ohmic devices are widely used in electronics despite not following Ohm's law.
diagram_description: Three V-I curve plots showing (1) curved non-linear relationship, (2) asymmetric diode characteristic, (3) non-unique multi-valued curve for GaAs
subject: physics

summary: Resistivity arises from electron collisions with ions: ρ = m/(ne²τ), where τ is the average relaxation time between collisions and n is electron density. This formula connects macroscopic Ohm's law (E = ρj) to microscopic electron dynamics (v_d = eEτ/m). At higher temperature, collision frequency increases, explaining increased resistivity in metals.
diagram_description: Electron trajectory showing collisions with fixed ions and average drift velocity superimposed
subject: physics

concept_name: Comparison of Drift Velocity with Other Velocities
summary: Drift velocity (~10⁻³ m/s for 1.5 A in copper) is extremely small compared to: (1) thermal speeds of atoms (~10² m/s); (2) speed of propagation of the electric field (~3×10⁸ m/s). Despite slow drift, current is established quickly because the field propagates instantly, and large electron density carries the current.
subject: physics

summary: The Lorentz force describes the total electromagnetic force on a moving charge: F = q(E + v × B). The magnetic component depends on velocity and is perpendicular to both v and B, producing zero force for parallel/antiparallel motion. This fundamental law governs all electromagnetic interactions of charged particles.
diagram_description: Vector diagram showing velocity, magnetic field, and resulting force direction using right-hand rule
subject: physics

summary: A moving charge q with velocity v in magnetic field B experiences force F = q(v × B), perpendicular to both. The force vanishes when v and B are parallel, and has maximum magnitude qvB when perpendicular. The direction follows the right-hand rule for cross products.
diagram_description: 3D vector diagram showing charge velocity, magnetic field direction, and resulting force using right-hand rule
subject: physics

summary: The total Lorentz force combines electric force (qE) and magnetic force (q(v × B)). Electric force is independent of velocity and does work on the charge. Magnetic force is velocity-dependent and perpendicular to motion, doing no work but changing direction.
diagram_description: Vector diagram decomposing total force into electric and magnetic components
subject: physics

summary: A straight conductor of length l carrying current I in external magnetic field B experiences force F = Il × B. The force is perpendicular to both current direction and field, with magnitude IlB sin(θ). This principle enables motors and electromagnetic devices.
diagram_description: Diagram showing current-carrying wire in magnetic field with force vector and geometry
subject: physics

concept_name: Motion of Charged Particles in Magnetic Fields
subtopic_names: Circular Motion in Uniform Magnetic Field
Tesla Unit and Magnetic Field Magnitude
summary: Charged particles moving perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field undergo circular motion due to perpendicular magnetic force acting as centripetal force. The radius and frequency depend on particle properties and field strength. Particles with velocity components parallel to B undergo helical motion.
diagram_description: Circular and helical paths of charged particles in uniform magnetic field with labeled radius and velocity components
subject: physics

concept_name: Circular Motion in Uniform Magnetic Field
parent_concept_name: Motion of Charged Particles in Magnetic Fields
summary: When a charged particle moves perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field, the magnetic force provides centripetal acceleration, causing circular motion. The radius r = mv/(qB) depends on momentum and field strength. Larger momentum or weaker field produces larger circular paths.
diagram_description: Top-view diagram of circular path with center, radius, velocity vector, and magnetic field direction marked

builds_upon.concept_name: Motion of Charged Particles in Magnetic Fields

subject: physics

parent_concept_name: Motion of Charged Particles in Magnetic Fields
summary: The cyclotron frequency ω = qB/m is the angular frequency at which a charged particle orbits in a magnetic field. Remarkably, this frequency is independent of the particle's speed or orbital radius. The period T = 2πm/(qB) depends only on particle properties and field strength.
diagram_description: Graph showing cyclotron frequency independence from particle speed with corresponding orbital radii

builds_upon.concept_name: Motion of Charged Particles in Magnetic Fields

subject: physics

parent_concept_name: Motion of Charged Particles in Magnetic Fields
summary: When a charged particle has velocity component v∥ parallel to the magnetic field and perpendicular component v⊥, it undergoes helical motion combining circular motion in the perpendicular plane with linear motion along the field. The pitch p = 2πmv∥/(qB) is the distance traveled parallel in one orbit.
diagram_description: 3D diagram showing helical trajectory with pitch, radius of helix, and field direction

builds_upon.concept_name: Motion of Charged Particles in Magnetic Fields

subject: physics

summary: Magnetic fields are produced by moving charges and currents. Both Biot-Savart and Ampère's laws relate magnetic field to current sources. These laws allow calculation of fields for standard geometries like wires, loops, and solenoids using superposition principles.
diagram_description: Comparative diagram of magnetic field patterns for straight wire, circular loop, and solenoid
subject: physics

summary: The Biot-Savart law gives the magnetic field dB from an infinitesimal current element: dB = (μ₀/4π)(I dl × r)/r³. The field is perpendicular to both current element and position vector, with magnitude proportional to current and inversely to distance squared. Integration over the full conductor yields total field.
diagram_description: Vector diagram showing current element, position vector, and resulting magnetic field direction
subject: physics

summary: Ampère's law states that the line integral of B around a closed loop equals μ₀ times the enclosed current: ∮B·dl = μ₀I. For symmetric configurations where B is tangential and constant, this simplifies to BL = μ₀I. The law applies only to steady currents.
diagram_description: Closed loop around current-carrying wire showing tangential magnetic field
subject: physics

summary: A long straight wire carrying current I produces a magnetic field B = (μ₀I)/(2πr) at perpendicular distance r. Field lines form concentric circles around the wire with direction given by the right-hand rule. Field strength decreases inversely with distance.
diagram_description: Top-view showing concentric circular field lines around current-carrying wire with current direction and field direction marked
subject: physics

summary: A circular loop of radius R carrying current I produces axial field B = (μ₀IR²)/(2(x² + R²)^(3/2)) at distance x along the axis. At the center (x = 0), B = μ₀I/(2R). The field pattern resembles that of a magnetic dipole at large distances.
diagram_description: Diagram of circular loop with axial field strength variation and field line pattern
subject: physics

summary: A long solenoid with n turns per unit length carrying current I produces uniform interior field B = μ₀nI directed along the axis and negligible exterior field. The field is independent of position inside and provides practical uniform field generation for many applications.
diagram_description: Cross-section and side-view of solenoid showing uniform interior field and exterior field cancellation
subject: physics

summary: Current loops produce magnetic moments and experience torque in external magnetic fields. A loop with N turns, current I, and area A has dipole moment m = NIA. In external field B, torque τ = m × B tends to align the dipole, analogous to electric dipoles in electric fields.
diagram_description: Diagram showing current loop, magnetic moment vector, and torque in external field at various orientations

builds_upon.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subject: physics

summary: Magnetic moment m = NIA is a vector quantity characterizing a current loop's strength as a magnetic dipole. For a single turn, m = IA with magnitude equal to current times area. Direction is given by the right-hand rule: curl fingers with current, thumb points along m.
diagram_description: Diagram of current loop with area vector and magnetic moment vector using right-hand rule
subject: physics

summary: A current loop in a uniform magnetic field experiences torque τ = NIABsin(θ) where θ is angle between moment and field. The torque arises from equal, opposite forces on current-carrying sides, creating a couple. Torque vanishes when loop aligns with field.
diagram_description: Diagram of rectangular loop in magnetic field showing forces on each side and resulting torque
subject: physics

summary: A magnetic dipole in external field experiences net torque driving alignment. Stable equilibrium occurs when dipole moment is parallel to field (θ = 0°); unstable when antiparallel (θ = 180°). Small perturbations from stable position produce restoring torques. This explains compass alignment.
diagram_description: Diagram showing dipole orientations with torque arrows indicating stable and unstable equilibrium positions
subject: physics

summary: At distances far from a current loop (x >> R), the field resembles that of a point magnetic dipole with moment m = IA. The axial field becomes B ≈ (μ₀/4π)(2m/x³), analogous to electric dipole field. A planar loop is the elementary magnetic dipole; no magnetic monopoles exist.
diagram_description: Diagram comparing near-field loop pattern with far-field dipole-like pattern and field equations
subject: physics

summary: Two parallel conductors carrying currents experience magnetic forces: parallel currents attract, antiparallel currents repel. Force per unit length is f = (μ₀I₁I₂)/(2πd) where d is separation. This principle defines the ampere and enables current measurement.
diagram_description: Diagram of two parallel wires with field from one creating force on the other, showing attraction/repulsion
subject: physics

summary: Parallel currents (same direction) attract with force per unit length f = (μ₀I₁I₂)/(2πd). Antiparallel currents (opposite direction) repel with same magnitude. The force arises because each wire's field exerts force on the other's current, consistent with Newton's third law.
diagram_description: Two parallel wires with current directions and resulting attractive or repulsive forces indicated
subject: physics

summary: The ampere (A) is defined as the steady current in two parallel conductors 1 meter apart in vacuum that produces force of 2 × 10⁻⁷ newtons per meter of length. This definition operationally links electromagnetic force to the standard unit of electric current.
diagram_description: Diagram of two parallel conductors 1 m apart with current and force measurement apparatus
subject: physics

summary: The moving coil galvanometer detects current by measuring torque on a coil in a radial magnetic field. Deflection φ = (NAB/k)I is proportional to current. Galvanometers form the basis for ammeters and voltmeters through addition of shunt and series resistances.
diagram_description: Cross-section diagram of moving coil galvanometer showing coil, magnet, scale, and pointer
subject: physics

summary: A moving coil galvanometer consists of a multi-turn coil free to rotate in a uniform radial magnetic field. Current through the coil produces torque τ = NIAB, balanced by spring restoring torque kφ. Deflection φ is proportional to current and displayed on a calibrated scale.
diagram_description: Detailed cross-section showing coil, permanent magnets, soft iron core, spring mechanism, and scale
subject: physics

summary: A galvanometer becomes an ammeter by connecting a small shunt resistance rₛ in parallel. Most current bypasses through the shunt while full-scale current flows through the galvanometer. Effective resistance becomes approximately rₛ, allowing accurate current measurement with minimal circuit disruption.
diagram_description: Circuit diagram showing galvanometer with shunt resistor in parallel configuration
subject: physics

summary: A galvanometer becomes a voltmeter by connecting a large multiplier resistance R in series. Most voltage drops across the resistor while allowing only full-scale current through the galvanometer. Voltmeter resistance becomes approximately R, providing high impedance to minimize current draw from circuits.
diagram_description: Circuit diagram showing galvanometer with series multiplier resistance for voltmeter configuration
subject: physics

summary: Current sensitivity Sᵢ = φ/I = NAB/k is deflection per unit current. Voltage sensitivity Sᵥ = φ/V = (NAB/k)(1/R) depends inversely on series resistance R. Doubling turns N doubles current sensitivity but voltage sensitivity remains unchanged if resistance scales proportionally.
diagram_description: Graph showing linear relationship between current/voltage and deflection for typical galvanometer
subject: physics

concept_name: Oersted's Experiment and Magnetic Field Discovery
summary: Hans Christian Oersted's 1820 demonstration showed that a current in a straight wire deflects a nearby compass needle, proving moving charges produce magnetic fields. Compass alignment was tangential to circles centered on the wire. Reversing current reversed needle orientation, establishing that currents create magnetic fields.
diagram_description: Historical diagram showing Oersted's apparatus with wire, compass needle, and field line pattern
subject: physics

parent_concept_name: Motion of Charged Particles in Magnetic Fields
summary: Two distinct right-hand rules determine magnetic field direction: (1) For straight wire, thumb points along current, fingers curl in field direction. (2) For circular loop, fingers curl with current, thumb points along axis in field direction. These conventions ensure consistent direction determination for the cross product in magnetic equations.
diagram_description: Hand diagrams demonstrating right-hand rule for straight wire and circular loop with labeled directions
subject: physics

summary: Permeability of free space μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ T·m/A is a fundamental constant in magnetic field equations. It appears in both Biot-Savart law and Ampère's law as the proportionality constant relating magnetic field to current sources. Its value is fixed by SI definition.
subject: physics

summary: A cyclotron exploits the fact that cyclotron frequency ω = qB/m is independent of particle speed. Alternating voltage synchronized to this frequency accelerates particles each pass, increasing their energy and orbital radius while maintaining the same frequency. This enables efficient acceleration to very high energies.
diagram_description: Diagram of cyclotron showing two semicircular dees, particle spiraling path, and voltage application points

builds_upon.concept_name: Motion of Charged Particles in Magnetic Fields

subject: physics

concept_name: Tesla Unit and Magnetic Field Magnitude
parent_concept_name: Motion of Charged Particles in Magnetic Fields
summary: The tesla (T) is the SI unit of magnetic field strength. One tesla is defined as the field strength that exerts one newton of force on a one-coulomb charge moving at one meter per second perpendicular to the field. Earth's magnetic field is approximately 3.6 × 10⁻⁵ T.
subject: physics

subtopic_names: Magnetic Field Lines and Their Properties
summary: A bar magnet behaves as a magnetic dipole with north and south poles that cannot be isolated. When cut in half, it produces two smaller dipoles rather than isolated poles. The magnetic field pattern is identical to that of a current-carrying solenoid, suggesting magnets arise from circulating atomic currents.
diagram_description: Iron filings pattern around a bar magnet showing field lines emanating from north pole and converging at south pole
subject: physics

summary: Magnetic poles are the regions of maximum magnetic field strength at magnet ends. Unlike electric charges, isolated magnetic poles (monopoles) do not exist in nature. Every magnet necessarily has both north and south poles; breaking a magnet produces two new dipoles.
diagram_description: Diagram showing a bar magnet with poles labeled, and a broken magnet producing two smaller magnets each with poles
subject: physics

summary: A bar magnet produces the same magnetic field pattern as a current-carrying solenoid, suggesting all magnetism arises from circulating currents (Ampere's hypothesis). At large distances, the axial field of a finite solenoid matches that of a bar magnet with equivalent magnetic moment. This analogy explains why cutting a magnet produces two smaller magnets with weaker properties.
diagram_description: Side-by-side field line patterns of a bar magnet and a current-carrying solenoid showing identical field geometry
subject: physics

concept_name: Magnetic Field Lines and Their Properties
summary: Magnetic field lines are continuous closed loops (unlike electric field lines) that form a visual representation of the magnetic field. The tangent to a field line at any point gives the field direction; the density of lines indicates field strength. Field lines never intersect, ensuring unique field direction at every point.
diagram_description: Magnetic field line pattern around a bar magnet with arrows showing direction and varying spacing to indicate field strength
subject: physics

summary: A magnetic dipole in a uniform external magnetic field experiences a torque that tends to align it with the field but no net force.
